Headline:  
PaTH: Data-Dependent Position Encoding Extends Expressivity of Transformer Attention Beyond RoPE

News Article:  
The limitations of large language models (LLMs) in handling sequential reasoning, idiomatic expression, and context-dependent language phenomena have been widely discussed in both technical and public discourse. As highlighted in recent analyses, LLMs are fundamentally constrained by their reliance on permutation-invariant attention mechanisms, which necessitate explicit position encoding to model structured domains such as language. This constraint underpins persistent challenges in tasks requiring nuanced state-tracking and sequential logic, as evidenced by the models’ difficulties with idiomatic and context-rich language use [Filtered Real-World Context: 2, 3].

The paper “PaTH: Data-Dependent Position Encoding with Accumulated Householder Transformations” (arXiv:2505.16381) addresses these foundational limitations by introducing PaTH, a position encoding scheme that leverages accumulated, data-dependent Householder transformations. Unlike the prevailing rotary position embedding (RoPE) approach, which is data-independent and solely a function of relative position, PaTH parameterizes the attention logit using a cumulative product of input-dependent matrices. This formulation enables the attention mechanism to dynamically adapt to the input sequence, thereby enhancing the model’s capacity for state-tracking and sequential reasoning [Abstract, Introduction].

RoPE, the current de facto standard for position encoding in LLMs, operates by applying a rotation matrix to key and query embeddings, with the rotation angle determined by the difference in positions. The rotation matrix is block-diagonal and composed of two-by-two rotation matrices, which allows for efficient computation. However, RoPE’s data-independence imposes a theoretical ceiling on its expressivity: RoPE-based transformers are computationally constrained to the TC0 complexity class, equivalent to ordinary transformers with absolute position embeddings. Empirical studies have shown that RoPE-based models struggle with synthetic tasks that require sequential reasoning, such as flip-flop language modeling and certain state-tracking benchmarks [Introduction].

PaTH addresses these expressivity limitations by introducing a data-dependent mechanism. In PaTH, the attention logit is parameterized as a bilinear form involving a matrix Hij, which is computed as a cumulative product of Householder-like matrices along the path between two positions in the sequence. Each Householder matrix in this product is a function of the input data, possessing an identity-plus-rank-one structure. This approach allows PaTH to capture cumulative transformations between positions, enabling the attention mechanism to adaptively encode state information as it traverses the sequence [Abstract, Introduction].

From a computational complexity perspective, the introduction of data-dependent Householder transformations in PaTH extends the theoretical capabilities of transformer architectures. The authors demonstrate that a constant-layer PaTH-based transformer can solve an NC1-complete problem under AC0 reductions, thereby extending the computational power of transformers beyond the TC0 class, assuming TC0 ≠ NC1. This result situates PaTH-based transformers in a higher complexity class than their RoPE-based counterparts, with direct implications for the types of sequential reasoning tasks these models can address [Introduction].

To ensure the scalability of PaTH, the authors develop a FlashAttention-style blockwise algorithm for efficient parallel training. This algorithm exploits a compact representation for products of Householder matrices, minimizing I/O cost and enabling hardware-efficient implementation. The PaTH attention layer is made available as part of the FLASHLINEARATTENTION library, facilitating adoption and further experimentation by the research community [Abstract, Introduction].

Empirical evaluation of PaTH demonstrates its effectiveness on both synthetic and real-world language modeling tasks. On targeted synthetic benchmarks designed to test state-tracking and sequential reasoning, PaTH-based transformers outperform RoPE-based models. In moderate-scale language modeling experiments with 760M-parameter transformers, PaTH not only surpasses RoPE but also outperforms the Forgetting Transformer, a recent baseline that modulates attention logits via a data-dependent additive term. Notably, combining PaTH with the Forgetting Transformer yields further improvements, and models trained with this hybrid approach generalize well beyond the training sequence length [Abstract, Introduction].

The persistent challenges faced by LLMs in capturing complex sequential dependencies and context-sensitive language phenomena underscore the importance of advancing position encoding schemes. PaTH’s data-dependent, cumulative transformation approach represents a significant step toward overcoming the theoretical and empirical limitations of existing attention mechanisms. For researchers and practitioners seeking a comprehensive understanding of the technical innovations and empirical results underpinning these findings, the full research paper provides detailed derivations, algorithmic insights, and experimental evidence.

Access the research paper at https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.16381.pdf to examine the technical foundations and implications of PaTH in advancing the expressivity of transformer-based language models.

End of News Article.

These limitations are rooted in the architectural primitives of modern AI systems, particularly the attention mechanism, which is inherently permutation-invariant and thus requires explicit position encoding to model structured domains such as language [Abstract][Introduction]. The prevailing approach, rotary position encoding (RoPE), has become the de facto standard in state-of-the-art LLMs, yet empirical and theoretical analyses reveal that RoPE-based transformers remain computationally constrained and struggle with tasks requiring sequential reasoning and state tracking [Abstract][Introduction].

The core contribution of PaTH is a data-dependent position encoding mechanism, wherein the transformation between sequence elements is a function of the input data, rather than solely their relative positions. This approach enables the attention mechanism to dynamically adapt to the input, thereby enhancing its capacity to model complex sequential dependencies.

Technically, RoPE operates by applying a rotation matrix to key and query embeddings, with the rotation angle determined by the difference in positions. This matrix is block-diagonal and data-independent, resulting in attention logits of the form \( q_i^\top R_{i-j} k_j \) [Introduction]. While this formulation is computationally efficient, it restricts the model’s expressivity to the TC0 complexity class, as demonstrated in recent work [7][46]. Empirical evidence further indicates that RoPE-based transformers exhibit difficulty on synthetic tasks such as flip-flop language modeling and state-tracking, which are indicative of broader limitations in real-world language understanding [38][48][Introduction].

PaTH addresses these constraints by parameterizing the attention logit as \( q_i^\top H_{ij} k_j \), where \( H_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d} \) is constructed as a cumulative product of data-dependent matrices along the path between positions \( j \) and \( i \) [Abstract][Introduction]. Each matrix in this product has a Householder-like identity-plus-rank-one structure, allowing the transformation to be sensitive to the input data. This formulation enables PaTH to capture cumulative transformations between positions, thereby facilitating dynamic adaptation to the input and improved performance on state-tracking tasks.

This parallel algorithm minimizes I/O cost and enables efficient training of PaTH-based attention layers at scale. The implementation is made available as part of the FLASHLINEARATTENTION library [Introduction].

The implications of these findings are significant for the ongoing development of LLMs and AI systems more broadly. original_title: AI gets a private tutor for learning human preferences more accurately
abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a powerful paradigm to align language models with human preferences using pairwise comparisons. However, its binary win-or-loss supervision often proves insufficient for training small models with limited capacity. Prior works attempt to distill information from large teacher models using behavior cloning or KL divergence. These methods often focus on mimicking current behavior and overlook distilling reward modeling. To address this issue, we propose Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD), which introduces an auxiliary reward from the value function of the teacher model to provide a soft guide. This auxiliary reward is formulated to satisfy potential-based reward shaping, ensuring that the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO are preserved. TVKD can be integrated into the standard DPO training framework and does not require additional rollouts. Our experimental results show that TVKD consistently improves performance across various benchmarks and model sizes.
introduction:
Introduction

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [24] is a powerful paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. Unlike Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) [22], DPO enables efficient learning through pairwise comparisons, without requiring additional rollouts and reward model training. However, since DPO supervision is provided only in terms of binary win-or-lose preferences over entire sequences, it becomes difficult to evaluate how good a given response is. This coarse-grained feedback offers limited insight into the magnitude or quality of preference, making it challenging to assess fine-grained improvements or to interpret alignment quality beyond simple pairwise comparison. In small language models (SLMs) which have limited capacity and language understanding, this issue becomes more crucial [9, 16].

To address this issue, several methods have attempted to leverage the rich information from large teacher models through diverse ways such as KL-divergence [9, 16] or online rollouts [30, 2]. However, most prior works focus on imitating the behavior of the teacher, without modeling its long- term reward structure. This issue has been widely discussed in the inverse RL [1, 33, 21] literature, where it is known that mimicking behavior without reward modeling can lead to compounding errors and poor generalization. Moreover, prior methods often disregard the potential conflict between the reinforcement learning (RL) objective and teacher information, leading to misalignment with the intended preference signal in datasets. For example, simply integrating KL-divergence with the teacher in the RL objective may result in conflict between following the teacher policy and optimizing

∗Co-supervising authors.

for human preferences in the offline dataset. This undermines the original intent of DPO, which is to align policies with human preferences expressed in data.

In this work, we propose Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD), a method that integrates soft reward labels from the teacher without interfering with the DPO reward structure and its global optimal policy. The key idea is to introduce a novel auxiliary reward term that leverages the value function of the teacher. These value functions provide estimates of the value function that serves as a proxy for the internal reward modeling of the teacher model. We add this value function to the RL objective in a form that satisfies Potential-based Reward shaping (PBRS) [20]. This formula theoretically guarantees that the comparison of the action-level Q-functions will not change, maintaining the optimal policy. We introduce these value functions as a soft sequence-level reward, enabling DPO to account not just for which sequence wins, but by how much. This adds more informative supervision to the originally binary feedback. Practically, it can be integrated into the DPO loss with minimal modification, providing a simple yet effective way to incorporate internal signals from the teacher model.

We demonstrate that TVKD achieves strong performance across multiple benchmarks for preference distillation, including MT-Bench [31], AlpacaEval [6], and the Open LLM Leaderboard [8]. Notably, our method requires no additional rollouts and only leverages teacher outputs on an existing offline DPO dataset, making it compatible with the standard training frameworks of DPO. In addition, we demonstrate that TVKD performs consistently across student models ranging from 0.5B to 3B parameters, and remains robust under a wide range of ablation.

Our main contributions are summarized as follows:

We introduce Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation, a preference distillation method that leverages the value function of the teacher as a proxy for its reward model.

We present a form of auxiliary reward term that satisfies potential-based reward shaping, suggesting a method for adding teacher information without conflict with the original DPO reward structure.

We empirically validate TVKD on multiple preference benchmarks, demonstrating consistent improvements across various student model sizes and datasets.

Headline:  
Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation Enhances Preference Alignment in Language Models

News Article:  

The persistent challenge of aligning generative AI systems with nuanced human preferences has direct implications for the reliability and societal impact of language models. Recent investigations have highlighted the amplification of harmful stereotypes and biases by generative AI, such as Stable Diffusion, which can exacerbate real-world inequities and erode public trust in AI systems [Filtered Real-World Context: Bloomberg, PwC]. These concerns underscore the necessity for robust alignment techniques that move beyond coarse, binary feedback and enable more granular control over model behavior, particularly as AI agents are increasingly deployed in enterprise and productivity contexts [Filtered Real-World Context: IntuitionLabs].

Addressing these alignment limitations, the newly proposed Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD) method introduces a mechanism for distilling preference information from large teacher models into smaller student models, while preserving the global reward structure and optimal policy of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [Abstract, Introduction]. TVKD leverages the value function of the teacher model to provide an auxiliary reward signal, formulated to satisfy potential-based reward shaping (PBRS). This approach enables the integration of soft, sequence-level reward information into the DPO training framework, without requiring additional rollouts or disrupting the original DPO objective [Abstract, Introduction].

The principal contributions of TVKD are threefold. First, it introduces a preference distillation method that utilizes the teacher’s value function as a proxy for its internal reward model. Second, it presents an auxiliary reward term that is compatible with potential-based reward shaping, ensuring that the integration of teacher-derived information does not conflict with the DPO reward structure. Third, empirical validation demonstrates that TVKD consistently improves performance across multiple preference benchmarks and a range of student model sizes, using only teacher outputs on existing offline DPO datasets [Abstract, Introduction].

From a technical perspective, DPO has emerged as an efficient paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences, relying on pairwise comparisons rather than the more resource-intensive reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) [Introduction]. However, DPO’s reliance on binary win-or-lose supervision over entire sequences limits its capacity to capture the magnitude or quality of preferences, particularly in small language models (SLMs) with constrained representational power [Abstract, Introduction]. This limitation impedes the fine-grained assessment of model responses and the interpretability of alignment quality.

Prior attempts to address these deficiencies have focused on distilling information from large teacher models using methods such as KL-divergence or behavior cloning [Introduction]. However, these approaches primarily target behavioral imitation and often neglect the underlying reward modeling, a shortcoming well-documented in the inverse reinforcement learning literature [Introduction]. Furthermore, the integration of teacher information into the RL objective can introduce conflicts with the intended preference signals in the dataset, undermining the alignment goals of DPO [Introduction].

TVKD circumvents these issues by introducing an auxiliary reward derived from the teacher’s value function, which serves as a proxy for the teacher’s internal reward modeling [Abstract, Introduction]. This auxiliary reward is incorporated into the RL objective in a manner that satisfies the theoretical requirements of potential-based reward shaping, thereby preserving the optimal policy and global reward structure of DPO [Abstract, Introduction]. The result is a soft, sequence-level reward signal that augments the binary feedback of DPO, enabling more informative supervision without necessitating additional rollouts or modifications to the standard DPO training pipeline [Abstract, Introduction].

Empirical results substantiate the efficacy of TVKD across multiple preference distillation benchmarks, including MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, and the Open LLM Leaderboard [Abstract, Introduction]. The method demonstrates consistent improvements in student models ranging from 0.5B to 3B parameters and exhibits robustness across a wide range of ablation studies [Abstract, Introduction]. Notably, TVKD’s compatibility with existing DPO datasets and training frameworks facilitates its adoption without incurring additional computational overhead [Abstract, Introduction].

As generative AI systems continue to permeate high-stakes domains, the need for alignment techniques that can reliably capture and operationalize human preferences becomes increasingly urgent. TVKD represents a methodologically distinct approach to preference distillation, offering a pathway to more interpretable and controllable language model behavior.

For a comprehensive technical exposition and empirical analysis, readers are encouraged to consult the full research paper: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.16965.pdf.

End of News Article.

Recent investigations, such as those highlighted by Bloomberg, have demonstrated that generative AI models—including Stable Diffusion—can amplify racial and gender stereotypes beyond real-world baselines, underscoring the limitations of current alignment techniques in mitigating harmful outputs. TVKD augments the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) framework by incorporating an auxiliary reward term that satisfies potential-based reward shaping, thereby preserving the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO. Empirical validation demonstrates that TVKD consistently improves performance across multiple preference benchmarks and model sizes, without requiring additional rollouts or deviation from standard DPO training protocols ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]).

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has emerged as a paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences using pairwise comparisons, circumventing the need for additional rollouts and explicit reward model training as required in Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF). However, DPO’s reliance on binary win-or-lose supervision over entire sequences introduces a granularity bottleneck: it fails to capture the magnitude or quality of preference, particularly in SLMs with limited representational capacity ([Introduction]). This coarse feedback impedes the evaluation of response quality and the interpretation of alignment beyond simple pairwise outcomes.

Prior attempts to address this limitation have focused on distilling information from large teacher models via behavior cloning or KL-divergence. These approaches primarily target behavioral imitation, neglecting the underlying reward modeling that governs long-term policy optimization. The inverse reinforcement learning literature has established that such imitation, absent explicit reward modeling, can result in compounding errors and poor generalization ([Introduction]). Furthermore, integrating teacher information directly into the RL objective—such as through KL-divergence—can introduce conflicts between the teacher policy and the human preference signals encoded in offline datasets, undermining the alignment intent of DPO.

TVKD addresses these deficiencies by introducing an auxiliary reward derived from the teacher model’s value function. This value function serves as a soft, sequence-level reward signal, providing a more informative supervisory signal than binary feedback alone. The auxiliary reward is formulated to satisfy potential-based reward shaping (PBRS), a property that ensures the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO remain intact ([Abstract], [Introduction]). By integrating the teacher’s value function into the DPO loss, TVKD enables the student model to account not only for which sequence is preferred, but also for the degree of preference, thereby enriching the learning signal without altering the fundamental DPO objective.

Empirical results substantiate the efficacy of TVKD across multiple benchmarks, including MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, and the Open LLM Leaderboard. The method demonstrates consistent improvements in preference distillation for student models ranging from 0.5B to 3B parameters and exhibits robustness across a range of ablation studies ([Abstract], [Introduction]). Notably, TVKD operates without the need for additional rollouts, relying solely on teacher outputs from existing offline DPO datasets, which facilitates seamless integration into established DPO training pipelines.

The technical contributions of TVKD are threefold: (1) it introduces a preference distillation method that leverages the teacher’s value function as a proxy for reward modeling; (2) it presents an auxiliary reward term that satisfies potential-based reward shaping, ensuring compatibility with the DPO reward structure; and (3) it empirically validates the method’s effectiveness across diverse benchmarks and model sizes ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

Given the persistent real-world challenges of AI bias and the technical limitations of current alignment methods, TVKD represents a methodologically distinct approach for enhancing preference alignment in language models. For a comprehensive exposition of the theoretical framework, empirical results, and implementation details, readers are encouraged to consult the full research paper ([Citation]: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.16965.pdf).

original_title: New 3D benchmark leaves AI in knots
abstract:
We propose KNOTGYM, an interactive environment for complex, spatial reasoning and manipulation. KNOTGYM includes goal-oriented rope manipulation tasks with varying levels of complexity, all requiring acting from pure image observations. Tasks are defined along a clear and quantifiable axis of complexity based on the number of knot crossings, creating a natural generalization test. KNOTGYM has a simple observation space, allowing for scalable development, yet it highlights core challenges in integrating acute perception, spatial reasoning, and grounded manipulation. We evaluate methods of different classes, including model-based RL, model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought reasoning, and illustrate the challenges KNOTGYM presents. KNOTGYM is available at https://github. com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Figure 1: KNOTGYM is a visual reasoning knot manipulation environment. It includes three tasks: transforming complex knots to a simple loop (example in the center); tying a loop into complex knots (right pane); and converting one knot into another, given a goal knot image. KNOTGYM has a continuous visual observation space (left pane) and an action space of applying forces to contact points (right pane), abstracting the specifics of robot end effectors. The space of goals, specified using Gauss code, is a factorial of the number of crossings (#X), which creates a ladder of generalization. Each goal defines an easily testable equivalence class over a continuous set of states.
introduction:
Introduction

KNOTGYM is a knot manipulation environment to train and evaluate visual reasoning agents. Figure 1 illustrates KNOTGYM. The agent observes the world state as an image and needs to transform a knot into a topological goal by applying forces to rope segments. The goal is communicated to the agent by observing a secondary knot as an exemplar. KNOTGYM defines three tasks (Table 1), each characterized by its start state and goal. KNOTGYM is designed with several aspects in mind:

Research Challenges KNOTGYM evaluates spatial reasoning, action prediction, planning, and abstraction skills in a single environment. Solving a task requires analyzing the current rope configura- tion, planning on deforming it to achieve the goal, and mapping this plan to a sequence of continuous action predictions. Critically, KNOTGYM does not enforce one specific state as the goal – the goal is a large equivalence class of states. Goals are defined via Gauss code, a formal mathematical description of knot configuration based on its crossings. When the goal is communicated using an exemplar image, solving a task requires abstracting over the exemplar to identify the right set of actions to complete the task. We show that KNOTGYM is a significant challenge for contemporary methods.

Measurable Complexity Knots are well studied in mathematics, with formal descriptions like Gauss code and a measure of complexity in the number of crossings.1 Figure 1 shows various knot configurations with different numbers of crossings, illustrating the increasing complexity of more crossings. This measure of complexity enables to control the challenges of learning and evaluation.

Generalization Ladder The number of crossings creates a clear generalization ladder. This allows a clear train-test complexity split, where we train up to a certain level of complexity (i.e., number of crossings) and test if a model generalizes beyond this number. During training, this allows us to experiment with a natural learning curriculum, where the number of crossings in training examples is gradually increased. It also enables the study of self-improving bootstrapping processes in a visual domain, as recently proposed for arithmetic and maze-solving [Lee et al., 2025b].

Research Accessibility KNOTGYM is an accessible task particularly suited for studying extended visual reasoning in a laboratory environment, just as Pendulum for classic control and Countdown for reasoning in natural language [Gandhi et al., 2024]. We follow the implementation and design principles of OpenAI Gym [Brockman et al., 2016] to make the KNOTGYM as accessible for researchers as possible. This includes following the standard Gymnasium API [Towers et al., 2024] and supporting vectorized environments on multiple CPUs. The KNOTGYM environment and our baselines are available under the MIT license at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Table 1: The three tasks of KNOTGYM. We show the final observation assuming the goal is achieved. Correctly completing tasks does not require getting the exact exemplar configuration (right rope in each image pair), but a configuration with the same Gauss code, a notation that describes the abstract spatial characteristics of a knot. For example, the final observations in the tie row are both [1+,1-,2+,2-], despite their different appearances.

Task name

Description

Initial→ Final Observations

unknot

Untangle a knot into a simple loop

tie

Tie a goal knot from a simple loop

convert

Tie a new knot from an old knot

1While the number of crossings is a fitting measure of complexity to our manipulation task, the prime complexity measure of interest in mathematics is more likely the counting of crossing of irreducible knots (i.e., as part of the tabulation of prime knots). Under this perspective, all the knots in Figure 1 are equivalent, because they all can be reduced to a simple loop by applying a few (or many) Reidemeister moves. For us, the number of crossings is a more appropriate measure because we aim for manipulation and observation complexity.

KNOTGYM: A Quantifiable Benchmark for Visual Spatial Reasoning and Manipulation in Robotic Agents

News Article:  
The rapid expansion of the smart robots market, projected to reach USD 42.80 billion by 2030 with a CAGR of 21.5% from 2025 to 2030, underscores the increasing demand for advanced robotic systems capable of sophisticated task automation and visual reasoning [Filtered Real-World Context]. As robots are deployed in environments that are inaccessible or hazardous for humans, their ability to adapt to novel scenarios through advanced sensors and algorithms becomes essential [Filtered Real-World Context]. However, the development and evaluation of such adaptive capabilities remain a significant challenge, particularly in domains requiring acute perception, spatial reasoning, and grounded manipulation.

Addressing this gap, the KNOTGYM environment introduces a structured and quantifiable platform for training and evaluating visual reasoning agents on complex rope manipulation tasks [Abstract][Introduction]. KNOTGYM is designed to assess an agent’s ability to perform goal-oriented manipulations based solely on image observations, with tasks defined along a measurable axis of complexity: the number of knot crossings. This approach enables a natural generalization test, as the factorial growth of possible knot configurations with increasing crossings provides a scalable and rigorous benchmark for both learning and evaluation [Abstract][Introduction].

KNOTGYM comprises three primary tasks: unknot (untangling a knot into a simple loop), tie (tying a goal knot from a simple loop), and convert (transforming one knot into another, given a goal knot image) [Abstract][Introduction][Table 1]. Each task is characterized by its initial and goal states, with goals specified using Gauss code—a formal mathematical notation that encodes the sequence and orientation of knot crossings. This abstraction allows for the definition of equivalence classes over continuous state spaces, such that achieving the goal does not require replicating the exact visual configuration of the exemplar, but rather matching its Gauss code [Abstract][Introduction][Table 1].

The technical architecture of KNOTGYM is notable for its simplicity and scalability. The observation space consists of continuous visual inputs, while the action space is defined by the application of forces to contact points on the rope, abstracting away the specifics of robot end effectors [Abstract][Figure 1]. This design isolates the core challenges of perception, spatial reasoning, and manipulation, enabling focused investigation of these capabilities without confounding factors. The environment supports vectorized execution on multiple CPUs and adheres to the standard Gymnasium API, facilitating integration with existing reinforcement learning pipelines and promoting research accessibility [Introduction].

A central feature of KNOTGYM is its explicit generalization ladder, constructed by varying the number of knot crossings. This enables controlled experiments in curriculum learning, where agents are trained on tasks of increasing complexity and evaluated on their ability to generalize beyond the training distribution [Introduction]. The factorial growth of the goal space with respect to the number of crossings provides a rigorous testbed for studying self-improving bootstrapping processes in visual domains, analogous to recent work in arithmetic and maze-solving [Introduction].

KNOTGYM’s evaluation protocol encompasses a range of methodological paradigms, including model-based reinforcement learning, model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought reasoning [Abstract]. Empirical results demonstrate that the environment presents substantial challenges for contemporary methods, highlighting persistent gaps in the integration of perception, planning, and action prediction [Abstract]. The use of Gauss code as a goal specification further emphasizes the necessity for agents to abstract over visual exemplars and reason about topological equivalence classes, rather than relying on pixel-level matching [Introduction][Table 1].

The broader implications of KNOTGYM extend to the development of robotic systems capable of robust adaptation in unstructured environments. As noted in recent analyses, the ability of robots to adjust to changing conditions through advanced perception and reasoning is a prerequisite for safe and effective deployment in real-world scenarios [Filtered Real-World Context]. By providing a quantifiable and accessible benchmark for these capabilities, KNOTGYM offers a valuable resource for the research community seeking to advance the state of the art in visual spatial reasoning and manipulation.

Researchers and practitioners interested in the technical details, experimental protocols, and baseline implementations are encouraged to access the full KNOTGYM research paper and codebase at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym [Abstract][Introduction][Citation]. The paper provides comprehensive documentation and serves as the primary resource for understanding the environment’s design, evaluation methodology, and implications for future research.

End of News Article.

original_title: How AI helps solve problems it doesnt even understand
abstract: Streamlining constraints (or streamliners, for short) narrow the search space, enhancing the speed and feasibility of solving complex constraint satisfaction problems. Traditionally, streamliners were crafted manually or generated through systematically combined atomic constraints with high-effort offline testing. Our approach utilizes the generative capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) to propose effective streamliners for problems specified in the MiniZinc constraint programming language and integrates feedback to the LLM with quick empirical tests for validation. Evaluated across seven diverse constraint satisfaction problems, our method achieves substantial runtime reductions. We compare the results to obfuscated and disguised variants of the problem to see whether the results depend on LLM memorization. We also analyze whether longer offline runs improve the quality of streamliners and whether the LLM can propose good combinations of streamliners.
introduction:
Introduction

Streamliners are certain constraints added to a constraint model to reduce the search space, thereby improving the feasibility and speed of finding solutions to complex constraint satisfac- tion problems. By incorporating domain-specific knowledge, streamliners can guide the con- straint solver, allowing it to bypass less promising areas of the search space. Gomes and Sell- mann [2004] introduced streamliners to speed up the constrained-based search for hard combina- torial design problems. Today, streamliners are a standard tool for speeding up constrained-based search. Streamliners are closely related to implied/redundant constraints, symmetry-breaking constraints, and dominance-breaking constraints; however, adding a streamliner may even cause the constraint model to become inconsistent.

Originally, streamliners were hand-crafted by researchers who used their theoretical insight to analyze the constrained model. However, progress has also been made on the automated gen- eration of streamliners [Spracklen et al., 2023] by systematically trying the effect of some atomic constraints, such as imposing specific constraints on integer and function domains, like enforcing odd or even values, monotonicity, and properties like commutativity, as well as facilitating spe- cific attributes in binary relations. These atomic restrictions are tested on thousands of problem instances, and those that show a good streamlining effect are systematically combined.

This paper proposes a different approach to automated streamliner generation that utilizes the generative capabilities (“creativity”) of Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate stream- liners, providing a neuro-symbolic prototype implementation StreamLLM, and rigorously tests

it on seven different constraint satisfaction problems using at least 310 CPU days1. Our ap- proach leverages the capabilities of LLMs to infer potentially effective streamliners, similar to an experienced researcher’s intuitive grasp of a problem. By integrating LLMs into the stream- liner generation process, we can potentially uncover unique and subtle patterns in the problem formulation and utilize them to speed up the solving process. To some extent, StreamLLM is closer related to a hand-crafted streamliner design by human experts rather than an automated bottom-up streamliner generation, the dominant strategy of the previous research [Spracklen et al., 2023].

Our system StreamLLM for streamliner generation combines queries to LLMs with quick em- pirical validation on small test instances solvable within seconds. The system generates effective constraints within minutes through engineered prompts and adaptive feedback based on stream- liner performance. This realtime approach allows streamliner benefits to be realized even for hard problem instances that would typically require hours to solve—any significant speedup can outweigh the brief time spent on streamliner generation.

We evaluate StreamLLM extensively across seven diverse constraint problems, including stan- dard benchmarks and a novel hypergraph coloring problem, using hundreds of instances spec- ified in MiniZinc. The problems are formulated in the MiniZinc constraint programming lan- guage [Nethercote et al., 2007]. We test StreamLLM with two state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet), different prompting variants, and different strategies for combining adaptive prompting with test runs on easy instances. Our results demonstrate remarkable effectiveness, with some streamliners reducing solving time by up to 99%.

To investigate whether these improvements stem from memorization or genuine problem un- derstanding, we test StreamLLM on disguised and obfuscated variants of the problems. The system’s continued strong performance, particularly on problems with no known streamliners in the literature, suggests real analytical capabilities rather than mere pattern matching.

Beyond the realtime setting, we explore offline approaches with extended training periods and larger instance sets, achieving even better results in some cases. We also investigate StreamLLM’s ability to generate effective combinations of streamliners, finding that this often outperforms single constraints. Analysis of the generated streamliners reveals an interesting mix—some mirror expert-designed constraints while others take novel yet highly effective approaches.

Our experimental results suggest that LLM-generated streamliners can meaningfully reduce runtime across diverse constraint satisfaction problems with minimal problem-specific tuning, pointing to promising applications of AI assistance in constraint programming for computationally challenging problems.

Part of this work appeared in preliminary and shortened form at the 1st International Workshop on Neuro-Symbolic Software Engineering (NSE) [Voboril et al., 2025c].

The source code and benchmark instances can be found on Zenodo [Voboril et al., 2025b].

Headline:  
LLM-Driven Streamliner Generation Accelerates Constraint Satisfaction Problem Solving

News Article:  
Efficient scheduling and resource planning remain persistent challenges for organizations operating under resource constraints, as highlighted in recent literature on mathematical optimization techniques for scheduling and planning. The ability to rapidly solve complex constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs) is a central concern in both academic research and real-world applications, where computational bottlenecks can impede operational efficiency and decision-making.

A recent study, available at arXiv (http://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.10268v3.pdf), introduces a novel approach to streamliner generation for CSPs by leveraging the generative capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Streamliners—additional constraints that narrow the search space—have long been recognized as a means to enhance the speed and feasibility of solving CSPs. Traditionally, these streamliners were either hand-crafted by domain experts or generated through systematic combinations of atomic constraints, both approaches requiring significant manual effort and extensive offline testing.

The core contribution of this work is the development and empirical evaluation of StreamLLM, a neuro-symbolic prototype that integrates LLMs into the streamliner generation process for problems specified in the MiniZinc constraint programming language. StreamLLM queries state-of-the-art LLMs (specifically GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet) to propose candidate streamliners, which are then subjected to rapid empirical validation on small, easily solvable test instances. This feedback loop enables the system to iteratively refine its streamliner proposals, achieving effective constraint generation within minutes.

The technical evaluation spans seven diverse constraint satisfaction problems, including standard benchmarks and a novel hypergraph coloring problem, with hundreds of instances formulated in MiniZinc. The system’s design allows for adaptive prompting and empirical testing, enabling the identification of streamliners that can reduce solving time by up to 99% in some cases. Notably, the approach is positioned as closer to expert-driven streamliner design than to previous automated, bottom-up methods, as it leverages the LLM’s capacity to infer effective constraints in a manner analogous to human intuition.

To rigorously assess whether the observed performance gains are attributable to genuine analytical capabilities rather than memorization, the study evaluates StreamLLM on obfuscated and disguised variants of the benchmark problems. The system maintains strong performance even on problems with no known streamliners in the literature, indicating that the LLM is not merely pattern-matching but is capable of substantive problem analysis.

Further analysis explores the impact of extended offline runs and the generation of composite streamliners. Results indicate that longer offline training periods and the synthesis of multiple streamliners can yield additional improvements in solving efficiency. The generated streamliners exhibit a spectrum of characteristics, with some mirroring expert-designed constraints and others representing novel, effective approaches not previously documented.

The implications of this research are significant for the constraint programming community. The ability to generate effective streamliners with minimal problem-specific tuning suggests that LLM-assisted streamliner generation could be broadly applicable to computationally challenging CSPs. This approach offers a pathway to reducing the manual overhead associated with streamliner design and to uncovering new, high-impact constraints that may not be readily apparent through traditional methods.

For researchers and practitioners seeking to examine the methodology, empirical results, and implementation details, the full research paper and associated resources—including source code and benchmark instances—are available on arXiv and Zenodo. These materials provide the necessary foundation for further exploration and validation of LLM-driven streamliner generation in constraint programming.

End of News Article.

original_title: Resolving to spend less time on your smartphone Understanding your travel habits can help, say researchers
abstract: Habits are a fundamental driver of human behavior. However, prior research has largely studied habits in isolation. This study investigates spatial and smartphone habits—two cornerstone habits in contemporary society—in tandem, revealing the multiplicative ways in which habitual processes can underlie human behavior. We provide evidence that smartphone habits are stronger in habitually traveled and visited spaces. We leverage a custom-designed app that logged mobility and app use while prompting map-based questionnaires of participants’ trips for two weeks (N=419 participants; n=27,446,977 GPS and app logs; n=7,226 trip questionnaires). Overall, app habit strength predicted higher likelihood of app use (vs. non-use) and more app use (when used) on more habitually travelled routes and in more habitually visited destinations. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most consistent for objective (vs. subjective) habit indicators. Social app habits were stronger and more context-independent than non-social app habits. Altogether, our findings reveal how distinct habitual processes can combine to shape daily behavior. Further, our method illustrates the potential of linking mobile sensing and surveying to measure habits in the real world.
introduction:
Habits are a direct and deep-rooted driver of human behavior1,2. By automating our cognition, habitual processes can make the world easier to navigate. Yet, they can also conflict with our goals3,4. For better and for worse, the degree to which behavior is performed habitually has powerful implications for how we engage with the world around us. Although people engage in a wide variety of habits in daily life, habits are typically studied in isolation5. By zooming out from this standard scope, we can reveal broader, multiplicative ways that habitual processes underlie real-world behavior across domains of everyday life.

Habitual behaviors are cued and performed automatically after being frequently rewarded in stable contexts2. In turn, the strength of a habit is indicated by the level of frequency (i.e., number of occurrences) and automaticity (i.e., lack of conscious processes)6. In contemporary society, spatial and smartphone habits act as cornerstone habits. Although a variety of factors can contribute to mobility choices and mobile phone use in a given situation, past research across disciplines indicates that habit is a leading predictor of both7,8. Critically, the interaction between these two types of habits is ideally suited for analyzing interactive habitual processes in daily life, as smartphone habits are uniquely activated across spaces9,10. Exploring their interactive relationship is also vital given the growing integration of mobility and technology: a convergence with implications for numerous facets of human behavior11–16.

Spatial habits are a prominent form of habitual behavior, as people often select and engage with their everyday spaces out of habit7,8. Scholars have extensively examined the role of habit in the selection of routes17–21, or how individuals travel from an origin to a destination via a particular mode of transportation (e.g., walking). Here, we focus on the role of habit in how people select routes as well as destinations, which together comprise the activity spaces that people inhabit in daily life22. Thus, spatial habits are comprised of places (i.e., destinations) and how individuals move between them (i.e., routes). Spatial habits can be cued by myriad factors (e.g., time, trip purpose)23. Research in cognitive geography has investigated spatial habits through the study of navigation, spatial cognition, and mental mapping. A notable example is anchor-point theory, which examines how people perceive and represent space through reference points like landmarks or neighborhoods24. Moreover, the centrality of spatial behavior in past habit research reflects the repetitive nature of daily mobility patterns identified in large-scale computational studies25,26. Spatial habits thereby have unique potential to operate as foundational habits: they determine the stable contexts that offer cues (or 'triggers') for other daily habits.

Smartphone habits are another dominant form of habitual behavior in modern society, as well as a heuristic case in habit research27. By bundling many possible behaviors (e.g., social media scrolling, podcast listening) into one handheld object, smartphones facilitate the development of a wide variety of activation cues (e.g., times, user activities), including personalized sets of cues tied to specific apps28. Accordingly, smartphone habits can be uniquely activated across a variety of spatial contexts9,10—challenging the presumed 'context-dependence' of traditional media habits and presumed role of location for cueing habits at-large2. However, some studies reveal that certain types of apps are also more likely to be used in certain types of places, as routes and destinations afford certain activities29,30. In this way, specific smartphone behaviors may develop implicit associations with specific types of spaces31,32—such as more habitually selected contexts33. In other words, spatial habits have the potential to make smartphone habits stronger.

To test this proposition, we examine whether more habitual apps are used disproportionately more in (1) habitually travelled routes and (2) habitually visited destinations. We test whether habitual apps can be interwoven with transportation habits by evaluating app use on routes. For example, app use and specific spaces may become embedded in scripts34: a habitual route (e.g., driving from home to work) can cue an app habit (e.g., listening to Spotify) at rehearsed points during the trip (e.g., traffic lights). These scripts may also be prompted by specific cues (e.g., time of day, social setting), thereby coupling spatial habits (e.g., the morning commute) and smartphone habits (e.g., listening to a news podcast) through sequences of cued actions. Additionally, we test the downstream influence of spatial habits on smartphone use by evaluating app use in destinations (e.g., the workplace). Since spaces chosen out of habit are more familiar35, habitually visited destinations may be more likely to contain familiar cues that activate app habits (e.g., checking a social media app while cleaning the kitchen). Altogether, we test whether app habits are activated more when individuals choose routes and destinations out of habit.

We further examine the robustness of the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits by exploring the distinctive role of social vs. non-social apps. Social apps provide a direct channel for social needs and norms to be activated habitually in daily life36. Based on the potency of social rewards in habit formation, along with the high proportion of social app usage in studies of overall smartphone usage37,38, social apps are expected to exhibit higher habit strength27,39. The theorized strength of social app habits may derive from their reliance on internal, non-spatial cues40. For example, people may habitually check social apps throughout the day in response to or in anticipation of new messages27,37. Thus, social app habits may be more likely to be context-independent: triggered across a variety of spatial contexts39.

Habits present a unique measurement challenge because they are triggered and performed with minimal conscious awareness throughout daily life41. Traditionally, habit research has centered on establishing the causal role of habit versus more conscious processes such as goals27. Here, moving beyond the question of whether habits or goals matter more, we focus on the real-world conditions—or contexts—under which habits become more likely to be activated. Accordingly, the current study sought to capture habits in the real world. By coupling GPS and app logs with map-based questionnaires, we collected objective and subjective indicators of spatial and smartphone habits and used those indicators to predict app use in context.

Headline:  
Interplay of Spatial and Smartphone Habits Revealed Through Large-Scale Mobile Sensing Study

News Article:  
The ubiquity of smartphones and the routinization of daily mobility have fundamentally reshaped the landscape of habitual human behavior. While the benefits and detriments of pervasive device use are widely acknowledged—ranging from physical discomfort and disrupted sleep cycles to increased feelings of loneliness, anxiety, and depression—there remains a critical need to understand how these cornerstone habits interact in real-world contexts. Recent research has highlighted that smartphones can serve both as tools for productivity and as distractions with potential negative implications for mental and physical health. However, the mechanisms by which spatial and smartphone habits coalesce to drive daily actions have not been systematically examined at scale.

A new study published in Scientific Reports addresses this gap by investigating the interactive dynamics of spatial and smartphone habits using a custom-designed mobile application. The research leverages a dataset comprising 27,446,977 GPS and app logs and 7,226 trip questionnaires from 419 participants over a two-week period. This methodological approach enables the simultaneous capture of mobility patterns and app usage, providing both objective and subjective indicators of habitual behavior in situ.

The study’s central contribution is the empirical demonstration that smartphone habits are amplified in habitually traveled routes and habitually visited destinations. Specifically, the strength of an individual’s app habit predicts both the likelihood of app use and the intensity of use when traversing familiar routes or occupying familiar destinations. This effect is most pronounced when measured using objective indicators of habit strength, such as frequency and automaticity, rather than subjective self-reports. The findings further delineate that social app habits exhibit greater strength and context-independence compared to non-social app habits, suggesting that internal, non-spatial cues play a dominant role in the activation of social app use.

From a technical perspective, the study operationalizes spatial habits as the selection of routes and destinations that constitute an individual’s activity space. These spatial habits are shaped by repeated exposure and are cued by factors such as time and trip purpose. The research draws on anchor-point theory and computational studies of mobility to contextualize the repetitive nature of daily movement patterns. By establishing that spatial habits provide stable contexts that cue other habitual behaviors, the study positions spatial habits as foundational in the hierarchy of daily routines.

Smartphone habits, in contrast, are characterized by their bundling of diverse behaviors—ranging from social media engagement to media consumption—within a single device. The study underscores that smartphone habits are uniquely activated across a variety of spatial contexts, challenging the traditional view that habits are predominantly context-dependent. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits is empirically tested by evaluating app use on habitual routes and in habitual destinations. The results indicate that habitual scripts—such as using a specific app at a particular point during a routine commute—are reinforced by the convergence of spatial and smartphone cues.

The research further interrogates the distinction between social and non-social app habits. Social apps, which facilitate the habitual activation of social needs and norms, demonstrate higher habit strength and are less tethered to specific spatial contexts. This is attributed to the potency of social rewards in habit formation and the prevalence of social app usage in overall smartphone behavior. The study posits that social app habits are more likely to be triggered by internal cues, such as anticipation of new messages, rather than by external spatial cues.

The methodological innovation of the study lies in its integration of mobile sensing and map-based surveying to capture real-world habitual processes. By combining objective GPS and app usage data with subjective trip questionnaires, the research provides a robust framework for measuring the activation of habits in context. This approach moves beyond traditional laboratory-based assessments of habit and goal-driven behavior, offering new insights into the conditions under which habits are most likely to be enacted.

The implications of these findings extend to the broader discourse on the impact of technology on well-being. The entanglement of spatial and smartphone habits may contribute to both the automation of daily routines and the persistence of behaviors that have adverse effects on physical and mental health, such as neck strain, disrupted sleep, and increased anxiety. Understanding the multiplicative effects of these habits is essential for developing interventions that address the negative outcomes associated with excessive smartphone use while leveraging the potential benefits of habitual behavior for productivity and well-being.

For a comprehensive analysis of the interactive mechanisms underpinning spatial and smartphone habits, and to access the full methodological and empirical details, readers are encouraged to consult the original research paper in Scientific Reports: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25174-2.pdf.

End of News Article.

Recent analyses highlight that excessive smartphone engagement can contribute to physical discomfort, such as neck strain and disrupted sleep cycles, as well as psychological effects including loneliness, anxiety, and depression. These findings underscore the necessity of understanding not only the isolated impact of smartphone or spatial habits, but also their potential interplay in shaping behavioral patterns and well-being in contemporary society.

A recent study published in Scientific Reports advances this discourse by systematically investigating the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits—two cornerstone habitual processes in modern life. Prior research has predominantly examined these domains in isolation, neglecting the possibility that their intersection may yield multiplicative effects on behavior. The present work addresses this gap by leveraging a custom-designed mobile application to collect high-resolution mobility and app usage data, coupled with map-based questionnaires, from 419 participants over a two-week period. The resulting dataset comprises over 27 million GPS and app logs and more than 7,000 trip questionnaires, enabling a nuanced analysis of how habitual processes co-occur and interact in real-world contexts.

The study’s principal contribution lies in its empirical demonstration that smartphone habit strength is amplified in habitually traveled routes and habitually visited destinations. Specifically, the likelihood of app use, as well as the intensity of use, increases when individuals are situated within spatial contexts that are themselves selected out of habit. This effect is most pronounced when considering objective indicators of habit—such as frequency and automaticity—rather than subjective self-reports. The research further distinguishes between social and non-social app habits, revealing that social app habits are both stronger and more context-independent, suggesting that internal cues may play a more significant role in their activation compared to non-social app habits.

From a technical perspective, the study operationalizes spatial habits as the repeated selection of specific routes and destinations, drawing on established frameworks in cognitive geography and computational mobility analysis. Spatial habits are conceptualized as foundational, providing stable contexts that serve as cues or triggers for other habitual behaviors. The methodology integrates continuous GPS tracking with app usage logs, enabling the identification of habitual routes and destinations at a granular level. Map-based questionnaires supplement these objective measures by capturing subjective perceptions of habit strength and context.

Smartphone habits are characterized by their bundling of diverse behaviors—such as social media engagement and media consumption—within a single device, facilitating the development of personalized activation cues. The study interrogates the context-dependence of these habits, challenging the traditional assumption that media habits are primarily cued by location. Instead, the findings indicate that while certain app behaviors are indeed associated with specific spatial contexts, social app habits exhibit a higher degree of context-independence, likely due to their reliance on internal, non-spatial cues such as anticipation of social interaction.

The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits is further elucidated through the concept of behavioral scripts, wherein habitual routes (e.g., commuting from home to work) become associated with specific app habits (e.g., listening to a podcast at a particular point in the journey). These scripts are reinforced by consistent cues, such as time of day or social setting, resulting in the coupling of spatial and smartphone habits through sequences of cued actions. The study’s analytic framework enables the quantification of these interactions, providing evidence that app habits are more likely to be activated in contexts that are themselves selected out of habit.

The implications of these findings extend to the measurement and modeling of habitual behavior in situ. By integrating mobile sensing with in-the-moment surveying, the study demonstrates a scalable approach for capturing both objective and subjective indicators of habit in naturalistic settings. This methodological advance addresses longstanding challenges in habit research, which has traditionally relied on retrospective self-report and laboratory-based paradigms that may not accurately reflect real-world behavior.

Given the documented associations between habitual smartphone use and adverse health outcomes—including physical discomfort and psychological distress—the elucidation of how spatial and smartphone habits interact is of particular relevance for researchers and practitioners concerned with the design of interventions and technologies aimed at promoting well-being. The study’s findings suggest that interventions targeting habitual behavior may benefit from considering the interplay between spatial and digital contexts, rather than addressing each domain in isolation.

original_title: Researchers reveal bias in a widely used measure of algorithm performance
abstract:
Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection

Normalized mutual information is widely used as a similarity measure for evaluating the In this paper, we argue that results performance of clustering and classification algorithms. returned by the normalized mutual information are biased for two reasons: first, because they ignore the information content of the contingency table and, second, because their symmetric normalization introduces spurious dependence on algorithm output. We introduce a modified version of the mutual information that remedies both of these shortcomings. As a practical demonstration of the importance of using an unbiased measure, we perform extensive numer- ical tests on a basket of popular algorithms for network community detection and show that one’s conclusions about which algorithm is best are significantly affected by the biases in the traditional mutual information.
introduction:
1 Introduction

A common task in data analysis is the comparison of two different labelings of a set of objects. How well do demographics predict political affiliation? How accurately do blood tests predict clinical outcomes? These are questions about how similar one labeling of individuals/items/results is to another and they are commonly tackled using the information theoretic measure known as mutual information, in which an experimental or computational measure of some kind is compared against a ground truth.

Mutual information works by asking of how efficiently we can describe one labeling if we already know the other [1]. Specifically, it measures how much less information it takes to communicate the first labeling if we know the second versus if we do not. As an example, mutual information is commonly used in network science to evaluate the performance of algorithms for network commu- nity detection [2]. One takes a network whose community structure is already known and applies a community detection algorithm to it to infer the communities. Then one uses mutual information to compare the output of the algorithm to the known correct communities. Algorithms that con-

sistently achieve high mutual information scores are considered good. We will use this application as an illustrative example later in the paper.

Mutual information has a number of appealing properties as a tool for comparing labelings. It is invariant under permutations of the labels, so that, for example, two divisions of a network into communities that differ only in the numbering of the communities will be correctly identified as being the same. It also returns sensible results in cases where the number of distinct label values is not the same in the two labelings. However, the standard mutual information measure also has some significant shortcomings, and two in particular that we highlight in this paper. First, it has a bias towards labelings with too many distinct label values. For instance, a community detection algorithm that incorrectly divides networks into significantly more groups than are present in the ground truth can nonetheless achieve a high mutual information score. A number of approaches for correcting this flaw have been proposed. One can apply direct penalties for incorrect numbers of groups [3] or subtract correction terms based on the average mutual information over some ensemble of candidate labelings [4, 5] or on the statistics of the contingency table [6, 7]. For reasons we discuss shortly, we favor the latter approach, which leads to the measure known as the reduced mutual information.

The second drawback of the mutual information arises when the measure is normalized, as it commonly is to improve interpretability. The most popular normalization scheme creates a measure that runs between zero and one by dividing the mutual information by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings being compared [8], although one can also normalize by the minimum, maximum, or geometric mean of the entropies. As we demonstrate in this paper, however, these normalizations introduce biases into the results by comparison with the unnormalized measure, because the normalization factor depends on the candidate labeling as well as the ground truth. This effect can be large enough to change scientific outcomes, and we provide examples of this phenomenon.

In order to avoid this latter bias, while still retaining the interpretability of a normalized mutual information measure, we favor normalizing by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling alone. This removes the source of bias but introduces an asymmetry in the normalization. At first sight this asymmetry may seem undesirable, and previous authors have gone to some lengths to avoid it. Here, however, we argue that it is not only justified but actually desirable, for several reasons. First, many of the classification problems we consider are unaffected by the asymmetry, since they involve the comparison of one or more candidate labelings against a single, unchanging ground truth. Moreover, by contrast with the multitude of possible symmetric normalizations, the asymmetric measure we propose is the unique way to normalize the mutual information without introducing biases due to the normalization itself.

More broadly, the asymmetric measure is more informative than the conventional symmetric one. Consider for instance the common situation where the groups or communities in one labeling are a subdivision of those in the other. We might for example label individuals by the country they live in on the one hand and by the town or city on the other. Then the more detailed labeling tells us everything there is to know about the coarser one, but the reverse is not true. Telling you the city fixes the country, but not vice versa. Thus one could argue that the mutual information between the two eshould be asymmetric, and this type of asymmetry will be a key feature of the measures we study.

Both drawbacks of the standard mutual information—bias towards too many groups and depen- dence of the normalization on the candidate labeling—can be addressed simultaneously by using an asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information as defined in this paper. In support of this approach we present an extensive comparison of the performance of this and other variants of the mutual information in network community detection tasks, generating a large number of random

test networks with known community structure and a variety of structural parameters, and then attempting to recover the communities using popular community detection algorithms. Within this framework, we find that conclusions about which algorithms perform best are significantly impacted by the choice of mutual information measure, and specifically that traditional measures erroneously favor algorithms that find too many communities, but our proposed measure does not. Code im- plementing our measure can be found at https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi.

Headline: Bias in Normalized Mutual Information Measures Alters Algorithm Evaluation in Classification and Community Detection

News Article:

The reliability of algorithmic evaluation metrics has direct implications for decision-making in domains ranging from healthcare to education. Recent polling data indicate that 55% of respondents perceive algorithms as having a significant influence on their decisions, with 28% reporting a moderate impact [Filtered Real-World Context, 2]. In high-stakes applications such as medical diagnostics, the presence of bias in algorithmic evaluation can lead to substandard clinical decisions and perpetuate disparities [Filtered Real-World Context, 3]. These concerns underscore the necessity for rigorous scrutiny of the metrics used to assess classification and clustering algorithms.

A new study, "Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection" [Citation], interrogates the foundational assumptions underlying one of the most widely used evaluation metrics: normalized mutual information (NMI). The authors demonstrate that NMI, despite its prevalence in clustering and community detection, is subject to two distinct sources of bias. First, NMI disregards the information content of the contingency table, and second, its symmetric normalization introduces spurious dependence on the algorithm output [Abstract]. These biases are not merely theoretical; the study provides empirical evidence that the choice of mutual information measure can significantly alter conclusions about which algorithms perform best in network community detection tasks [Abstract, Introduction].

Mutual information (MI) is a standard information-theoretic tool for comparing two labelings of a dataset, such as the output of a clustering algorithm against a ground truth. MI quantifies how much less information is required to describe one labeling given knowledge of the other [Introduction]. Its invariance under label permutations and its applicability to labelings with differing numbers of groups have made it a mainstay in the evaluation of community detection algorithms [Introduction]. However, the standard MI measure exhibits a bias toward labelings with an excessive number of distinct groups. For example, an algorithm that over-partitions a network can still achieve a high MI score, even when the partitioning is incorrect [Introduction].

To address this, prior work has proposed corrections, including penalties for incorrect group counts or adjustments based on the statistics of the contingency table. The latter approach yields the reduced mutual information, which the authors of the current study favor [Introduction]. However, the normalization of MI—intended to constrain its value between zero and one for interpretability—introduces a second, less-discussed bias. The most common normalization divides MI by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings, but this normalization factor depends on both the candidate and ground-truth labelings, introducing a spurious dependence that can materially affect evaluation outcomes [Introduction].

The authors propose an alternative: normalizing MI by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling alone. This asymmetric normalization eliminates the dependence on the candidate labeling and, according to the authors, is uniquely capable of avoiding normalization-induced bias [Introduction]. While asymmetry in the normalization may initially appear undesirable, the authors argue that it is justified in scenarios where one or more candidate labelings are compared against a fixed ground truth—a common situation in classification and community detection [Introduction]. Furthermore, the asymmetric measure is more informative in cases where one labeling is a subdivision of the other, as it captures the directionality of information content between coarse and fine labelings [Introduction].

The study substantiates its claims through extensive numerical tests on synthetic networks with known community structures. By applying a suite of popular community detection algorithms and comparing their outputs using both traditional and proposed mutual information measures, the authors demonstrate that the choice of metric can significantly influence which algorithms are deemed superior [Abstract, Introduction]. Specifically, traditional NMI measures tend to favor algorithms that over-partition the data, whereas the proposed asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information does not exhibit this bias [Abstract, Introduction].

These findings have immediate relevance for practitioners and researchers who rely on mutual information-based metrics to evaluate algorithmic performance. In domains where algorithmic decisions have tangible real-world consequences—such as medical diagnostics, educational assessment, and automated content moderation—the use of biased evaluation metrics can propagate errors and reinforce systemic disparities [Filtered Real-World Context, 3]. The study’s results also resonate with recent incidents in AI detection tools, where inaccurate algorithmic assessments have led to false accusations and public policy challenges [Filtered Real-World Context, 2].

Given the demonstrated impact of metric selection on algorithm evaluation, the authors provide open-source code implementing their proposed measure (https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi) [Introduction]. For researchers and practitioners seeking to ensure the validity of their algorithmic assessments, direct engagement with the full research paper is essential. The paper offers detailed theoretical analysis, empirical validation, and practical guidance for adopting unbiased mutual information measures in classification and community detection tasks.

End of News Article.

Mutual information (MI) is a standard information-theoretic tool for comparing two labelings of a dataset, such as the output of a clustering algorithm against a ground truth. original_title: Enabling small language models to solve complex reasoning tasks
abstract: Abstract While test-time reasoning enables language models (LMs) to tackle com- plex tasks, searching or planning in natural language can be slow, costly, and error-prone. But even when LMs struggle to emulate the precise rea- soning steps needed to solve a problem, they often excel at describing its abstract structure—both how to verify solutions and how to search for them. This paper introduces DISCIPL, a method for “self-steering” LMs where a Planner model generates a task-specific inference program that is executed by a population of Follower models. Our approach equips LMs with the abil- ity to write recursive search procedures that guide LM inference, enabling new forms of verifiable and efficient reasoning. When instantiated with a small Follower (e.g., Llama-3.2-1B or Qwen3-1.7B), DISCIPL matches (and sometimes outperforms) much larger models, including GPT-4o and o1, on challenging constrained generation tasks. Our work opens up a design space of highly-parallelized Monte Carlo inference strategies that outperform standard best-of-N sampling, require no finetuning, and can be implemented automatically by existing LMs.
introduction:
Introduction

Even as language models (LMs) are becoming increasingly proficient at reasoning tasks, progress has been “jagged” (Karpathy, 2024; Roose, 2025): today’s frontier models surpass experts at science and math reasoning (e.g., Hendrycks et al., 2021; Rein et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024b) yet still routinely struggle with counting, arithmetic, tic-tac-toe, metered po- etry, and other intuitively simple tasks (Ball et al., 2024; McCoy et al., 2023; Xu & Ma, 2024). For example, even very capable LMs have difficulty writing a coherent sentence under the constraints in Fig. 1, which are manageable for most proficient English speakers.

There is a growing consensus that many problems require a more deliberate, effortful style of thinking (Kahneman, 2011). However, an open question is how to structure inference so as to best leverage available computational resources. One popular approach performs in-context reasoning via serialized chain-of-thought (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025; OpenAI, 2024b). While highly flexible, reasoning via autoregressive generation is costly, slow, and can still produce unreliable outputs. On the other hand, structured inference methods like tree search (Silver et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2023) and sequential Monte Carlo (Lew et al., 2023; Loula et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2024) attain better parallelism and efficiency by coordinating test-time computation via external algorithms. However, these methods require significant hand-engineering and rely on pre-defined scorers or verifiers, limiting their applicability.

In this work, we propose a new meta-reasoning framework called DISCIPL in which lan- guage models themselves drive the decisions for how to structure inference-time computa- tion. In our approach, a Planner LM generates an ad-hoc problem specification (encoding its understanding of the task requirements) and inference procedure (encoding its plan for how to solve the task). Importantly, the specification and plan are implemented as inference programs that invoke Follower LMs, either generatively or as likelihood evaluators. By decomposing reasoning into planning and execution, our architecture preserves flexibility while enabling orchestration of highly efficient, parallel search patterns.
