Figure 1: Self-steering language models with probabilistic programs. LMs struggle with problems that combine generation and reasoning under constraints (top left, task from Yao et al., 2024). Popular approaches (top right) scale inference by search or sampling; however, even costly reasoning models do not always yield correct or fluent results (CoT example from o1, single-shot, first attempt). In our method (DISCIPL, bottom), a Planner LM writes an inference program that defines step-by-step com- putations to steer a population of Follower LMs. Our approach combines the benefits of serial and parallel methods: the Planner ensures correctness by construction, while the Followers collectively search for sequences with high probability. (See Fig. 7 for a detailed visualization.)

To test this approach, we evaluate DISCIPL on two domains: (1) COLLIE (Yao et al., 2024), a challenging constrained generation benchmark on which even very large LMs perform unreliably; and (2) PUZZLES, a custom dataset of difficult tasks involving poetry compo- sition, grant-writing, budgeting, and itinerary planning. We instantiate DISCIPL using a capable Planner LM to generate code (GPT-4o prompted with few-shot examples), and a small Follower LM to execute it (lightweight versions of Llama-3 or Qwen3). On para- graph tasks, this approach boosts accuracy well beyond the original capabilities of the Follower. Meanwhile, on more tightly constrained sentence and puzzle tasks, DISCIPL enables the Follower to surpass the Planner and approach the performance of powerful reasoning models like o1. Finally, we study how DISCIPL scales with the size and capabil- ities of the underlying Follower model, allowing our approach to benefit organically from advances in LM architecture and training methods.

Amidst a growing body of task-specific search and sampling methods for LMs (§2), we introduce a generalized approach. By using LMs to implement their own inference pro- cedures on-the-fly, DISCIPL yields efficient solutions to problems that would otherwise require non-trivial human engineering. We believe that this new combination of code gen- eration and probabilistic inference represents an exciting step in test-time scaling.

Rather than relying on fixed, hand-engineered search or sampling algorithms, DISCIPL leverages a Planner LM to generate a task-specific inference program, which is then executed by a population of Follower LMs. This architecture decomposes reasoning into planning and execution, allowing LMs to write recursive search procedures that guide inference and enable new forms of verifiable and efficient reasoning. Notably, when instantiated with small Follower models such as Llama-3.2-1B or Qwen3-1.7B, DISCIPL matches or outperforms much larger models, including GPT-4o and o1, on challenging constrained generation tasks ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

The technical motivation for DISCIPL arises from the observation that, while frontier LMs have demonstrated proficiency in science and math reasoning, they continue to struggle with tasks that combine generation and reasoning under constraints—such as counting, arithmetic, and constrained sentence construction ([Introduction]). Existing inference-time methods, including serialized chain-of-thought and structured inference via tree search or sequential Monte Carlo, either incur high computational costs or require significant hand-engineering and pre-defined verifiers. These approaches are often slow, costly, and error-prone, and do not generalize efficiently to new tasks ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

DISCIPL addresses these challenges by allowing the Planner LM to generate an ad-hoc problem specification and an inference procedure that encodes its understanding of the task requirements. The specification and plan are implemented as inference programs that invoke Follower LMs, either generatively or as likelihood evaluators. This design enables orchestration of highly efficient, parallel search patterns, combining the flexibility of serial reasoning with the efficiency of parallel search ([Abstract], [Introduction]). The Planner ensures correctness by construction, while the Followers collectively search for high-probability sequences, enabling efficient solutions to problems that would otherwise require non-trivial human engineering ([Abstract], Figure 1).

Empirical evaluation of DISCIPL was conducted on two domains: the COLLIE benchmark, which presents challenging constrained generation tasks, and a custom PUZZLES dataset involving poetry composition, grant-writing, budgeting, and itinerary planning ([Abstract], [Introduction]). DISCIPL was instantiated using a capable Planner LM (GPT-4o prompted with few-shot examples) and a small Follower LM (lightweight Llama-3 or Qwen3). On paragraph-level tasks, DISCIPL significantly improved the accuracy of the Follower LM. On tightly constrained sentence and puzzle tasks, DISCIPL enabled the Follower to surpass the Planner and approach the performance of advanced reasoning models such as o1 ([Abstract], [Introduction]). The study further demonstrates that DISCIPL’s performance scales with the size and capabilities of the underlying Follower model, allowing the approach to benefit organically from advances in LM architecture and training methods ([Abstract]).

By enabling LMs to implement their own inference procedures on-the-fly, DISCIPL introduces a generalized approach to test-time reasoning that does not require finetuning and can be implemented automatically by existing LMs ([Abstract]). This opens a design space of highly-parallelized Monte Carlo inference strategies that outperform standard best-of-N sampling and are applicable to a broad range of constrained generation problems ([Abstract]).

Given the prevalence of AI errors in real-world applications and the limitations of current inference methods, DISCIPL represents a significant technical advance in the structuring of inference-time computation for language models. For a comprehensive analysis of the DISCIPL framework, its empirical results, and its implications for the future of LM reasoning under constraints, readers are encouraged to access the full research paper ([Citation]).

End of News Article.

original_title: AI agents debate their way to improved mathematical reasoning
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved impressive results in complex reasoning and knowledge tasks, yet they often struggle with factual accuracy and logical consistency. Prior works have improved LLM performance using prompt-based techniques (e.g. chain-of-thought prompting and self-consistency) and post-hoc self-refinement, but these typically operate on a single model instance. Recently, multi-agent debate frameworks have emerged as a complementary approach, wherein multiple LLM agents propose answers and critique each other’s reasoning to reach consensus. Such a “society of minds” approach has been shown to significantly improve mathematical reasoning and reduce factual hallucinations. However, existing debate methods use homogeneous agents with simple majority voting, limiting their effectiveness. In this work, we propose Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD), a novel framework that extends multi-agent debate with (i) diverse specialized agents, (ii) dynamic debate routing, and (iii) a learned consensus mechanism. Each agent in A-HMAD is assigned a distinct role or expertise (e.g. logical reasoning, factual verification, strategic planning), enabling more comprehensive error-checking and perspective diversity than identical agents. A coordination policy dynamically selects which agents contribute at each round based on the question’s domain and the evolving debate state. To aggregate viewpoints, we introduce a consensus optimizer that learns to weight each agent’s vote according to its reliability and the confidence of its arguments. On six challenging benchmarks – including arithmetic QA, grade-school math (GSM8K), multifact question answering (MMLU), factual biography generation, and chess strategy – our A-HMAD consistently outperforms prior single-model methods and the original multi-agent debate baseline. Notably, A-HMAD achieves 4–6% absolute accuracy gains over standard debate on these tasks, and reduces factual errors by over 30% in biography facts. We provide extensive ablations demonstrating the benefits of agent heterogeneity, additional debate rounds, and the learned consensus module. Our findings suggest that an adaptive, role-diverse debating ensemble can drive significant advances in LLM-based educational reasoning, paving the way for safer, more interpretable, and pedagogically reliable AI systems.
introduction: Large-scale pre-trained language models have revolutionized NLP, demonstrating remarkable prowess in tasks requiring knowledge and reasoning (Brown et al. 2020; Srivastava et al. 2023). Models like GPT-3 and its successors exhibit strong few-shot learning abilities (Brown et al. 2020) and can generate coherent multi-step reasoning chains when prompted appropriately (Kojima et al. 2022). Despite these gains, even state-of-the-art LLMs often produce hallucinations – confidently stated false facts – and illogical reasoning leaps, undermining their reliability (Lin et al. 2021; Ji et al. 2023). This is due in part to the noisy, unverified nature of web-scale training data and the lack of explicit reasoning verification in standard next-token prediction training (Ouyang et al. 2022; Ziegler et al. 2019). Improving the factual accuracy and reasoning soundness of LLM outputs has therefore become a pressing research goal (Lee et al. 2022; Kadavath et al. 2022). A rich body of recent work has explored techniques to mitigate LLM reasoning and factual errors. Prompting strategies such as providing step-by-step exemplars or eliciting a chain-of-thought (CoT) have led to notable improvements in arithmetic and commonsense reasoning (Kojima et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022). For example, Wei et al. (2022) showed that CoT prompting enables models to break down problems into logical steps, and Kojima et al. (2022) found that even zero-shot prompts (e.g. “Let’s think step by step”) can induce multi-step reasoning in LLMs. Building on CoT, self-consistency decoding samples multiple reasoning paths and selects the most common answer, which boosts correctness by averaging out reasoning variance (Wang et al. 2022). Other works equip LLMs with scratchpads or external tools: Nye et al. (2021) introduced “scratchpad” memory for intermediate computations, and tool-use approaches allow models to consult calculators or knowledge bases to check facts (Yao et al. 2023; Schick et al. 2023). Orthogonal to prompting, learning-from-feedback methods like reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) fine-tune models to favor truthful, helpful outputs (Christiano et al. 2017; Stiennon et al. 2020), significantly reducing toxic or untrue generations (Siddiky et al. 2025). Likewise, post-hoc verifier models have been trained to check and correct a model’s answers (e.g. for math word problems Cobbe et al., 2021). Another promising direction is self-reflection and refinement, where the model iteratively critiques and improves its own output (Kawamura et al. 2003; Madaan et al. 2023). Madaan et al. (2023) showed that prompting an LLM to reflect on potential errors and then attempt a revised solution can yield more accurate results, echoing human problem-solving strategies (Renze and Guven 2024). Despite these advances, most techniques above involve a single model reasoning in isolation; the model may still overlook certain errors or fail to verify all facts due to its inherent biases or blind spots (Kadavath et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022). In contrast to single-model approaches, multi-agent frameworks draw inspiration from social or argumentative processes in which multiple experts collaborate or debate to reach a better answer (Michael et al. 2023). Early conceptual work by Minsky posited that intelligent behavior could emerge from a “Society of Mind” – a diverse collection of simpler agents. In the context of AI alignment and truthfulness, Khan et al. (2024) proposed AI debate, where two agents argue for opposing answers and a judge (human or AI) decides which argument is more convincing. The hope is that through adversarial debate, the truth would prevail because any false argument can be revealed by the opponent (Khan et al. 2024). While this work was theoretical, recent empirical studies have started to realize the benefits of multi-agent interactions. For example, Li et al. (2023) introduced a communicative multi-agent system (Camel) where agents with different roles (user, assistant, critic) collaborate to solve tasks. Similarly, MetaGPT by Hong et al. (2023) uses multiple specialized agent personas (e.g. PM, engineer, tester) to decompose and solve complex programming tasks, showing the power of role specialization. Focusing on language tasks, Kadavath et al. (2022) demonstrated that allowing two LLMs to debate from different viewpoints encourages divergent thinking and can improve answer quality. Parrish et al. (2022) found that when a less truthful LLM “debates” a more truthful LLM, the resulting answers tend to be more factual, suggesting that persuasion by a knowledgeable agent can correct the other’s misconceptions. These works hint that multi-agent interaction might overcome individual model limitations. However, some studies report mixed results; Chan et al. (2023) showed that a simple two-turn debate did not significantly help humans answer reading comprehension questions, implying that how debate is structured is critical. Most relevant to our work, Du et al. (2023) recently proposed a multi-agent debate framework for LLMs to improve both reasoning and factuality. In their approach, multiple instances of the same LLM are each prompted to generate an answer with reasoning. The agents then exchange their answers and critique each other’s reasoning over several rounds, before outputting a final consensus answer. This method, requiring only black-box access to the models’ outputs, was shown to significantly enhance performance on math word problems, strategic games, and factual QA, compared to single-agent baselines. Notably, Du et al. reported that debate reduces hallucinations: agents often identify and remove one another’s uncertain or inconsistent facts, leading to a final answer with more reliable information. The multi-agent debate can be seen as inducing an ensemble-like effect where multiple reasoning paths are explored in parallel and vetted against each other, yielding higher confidence when they converge. Zhang et al. (2023) further applied a debating agents idea for LLM-based evaluation, showing that a multi-agent debate can refine judgment of answers. Wu et al. (2024) used debates between a “student” and “professor” model to supervise and improve unreliable reasoning, while Zelikman et al. (2022) leveraged cooperative agents for embodied decision-making. These underline a growing consensus that multi-agent interactions can enhance an LLM’s performance and reliability. Despite the promise of multi-agent debate, there remain clear opportunities for improvement. First, prior debate frameworks largely use homogeneous agents – e.g. identical GPT-3.5 models with the same prompting style. This limits the diversity of perspectives; if all agents share similar strengths and weaknesses, certain errors may go unchallenged. Recent works in multi-agent collaboration emphasize heterogeneity as a key factor for success (Li et al. 2023; Hong et al. 2023), which suggests that introducing specialized agents could further improve the debate outcome. Second, existing methods rely on a simple majority vote or unanimous agreement for the final answer. This treats all agents as equally reliable, ignoring the possibility that some agents (or certain argument lines) might be more likely correct than others. A more nuanced consensus mechanism that weights agents by their past accuracy or the confidence of their justifications could lead to more robust final answers. Third, the debate protocol in prior work is static – agents alternate in a fixed order and number of rounds. In complex scenarios, a rigid protocol might be suboptimal: for example, if a particular agent has expertise most relevant to the query, the framework should allocate more opportunity for that agent to contribute, while bypassing irrelevant agents to save computation. Such dynamic agent routing or adaptive scheduling of debate rounds has not been explored in existing work. Finally, no prior study (to our knowledge) has integrated an explicit learning component into the debate loop – the interplay is handled by prompting alone. This raises the question of whether parts of the multi-agent debate process (e.g. how to aggregate answers) can be learned from data to further boost performance. In this paper, we propose a new framework called Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) that addresses the above limitations and substantially improves LLM factuality and reasoning via multi-agent debate. Our contributions are summarized as follows: (1) We design a heterogeneous agent ensemble where each agent has a specialized role or distinct model type, along with a dynamic routing strategy that activates different subsets of agents depending on the query type and intermediate debate outcomes. (2) We propose a learned consensus module that replaces simple majority voting for final answer selection, weighting agents based on features of their responses and confidence. (3) We thoroughly evaluate A-HMAD on six challenging benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art results for our base model, and significantly reducing hallucinations and incorrect facts compared to single-agent and prior debate baselines. (4) Through controlled ablations and qualitative case studies, we show why our components matter and how expert agents can correct others’ errors. Together, these advances move us closer to robust, truth-seeking AI systems that can reliably solve complex problems and self-correct errors.

The proliferation of AI-generated misinformation, including political propaganda and health-related falsehoods, has intensified scrutiny of large language models (LLMs) and their reliability in high-stakes domains. Recent analyses highlight the risks posed by LLMs’ failed approximations, which can result in the dissemination of unverified or misleading information, influencing public decision-making and undermining information integrity ([Filtered Real-World Context]: Psychology Today, IBM, Access Now). As LLMs are increasingly deployed in contexts where factual accuracy is paramount, the need for robust mechanisms to mitigate hallucinations and logical inconsistencies has become a central research concern.

Addressing these challenges, a new study introduces the Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) framework, which extends multi-agent debate architectures to improve both factual accuracy and reasoning soundness in LLM outputs ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]). Unlike prior debate frameworks that employ homogeneous agents and static protocols, A-HMAD incorporates three principal innovations: (i) a heterogeneous ensemble of specialized agents, (ii) dynamic debate routing, and (iii) a learned consensus mechanism. Empirical evaluation across six benchmarks—including arithmetic QA, GSM8K, MMLU, factual biography generation, and chess strategy—demonstrates that A-HMAD achieves 4–6% absolute accuracy gains over standard debate methods and reduces factual errors in biography generation by over 30% ([Abstract]).

The technical foundation of A-HMAD is motivated by persistent limitations in single-model and homogeneous multi-agent approaches. While LLMs such as GPT-3 and its successors have demonstrated strong few-shot learning and multi-step reasoning capabilities, they remain susceptible to hallucinations and illogical reasoning due to the noisy, unverified nature of their training data and the absence of explicit reasoning verification ([Introduction]). Previous mitigation strategies—including chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, self-consistency decoding, scratchpad memory, tool-use, and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)—have yielded incremental improvements but typically operate on a single model instance ([Introduction]). These methods, while effective in certain contexts, do not fully address the risk of unchallenged errors arising from model-specific biases or blind spots.

Multi-agent debate frameworks, inspired by the “Society of Mind” paradigm, have emerged as a complementary approach. In these systems, multiple LLM agents independently propose answers and critique each other’s reasoning, with the goal of reaching a more reliable consensus ([Introduction]). Prior empirical studies have shown that such debate protocols can reduce hallucinations and improve answer quality by enabling agents to identify and correct each other’s errors ([Introduction]). However, existing implementations predominantly utilize homogeneous agents—identical models with uniform prompting—and rely on simple majority voting for consensus, which limits the diversity of perspectives and fails to account for agent-specific reliability ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

A-HMAD addresses these deficiencies through three core components. First, it introduces agent heterogeneity: each agent is assigned a distinct role or area of expertise, such as logical reasoning, factual verification, or strategic planning ([Abstract]). This specialization enables more comprehensive error-checking and perspective diversity, as agents with different strengths can challenge and correct each other’s outputs. Second, A-HMAD employs dynamic debate routing, wherein a coordination policy selects which agents participate in each debate round based on the question’s domain and the evolving state of the discussion ([Abstract]). This adaptive scheduling allows the framework to allocate computational resources efficiently and prioritize relevant expertise. Third, the framework replaces majority voting with a learned consensus optimizer that weights each agent’s vote according to its historical reliability and the confidence of its arguments ([Abstract]). This mechanism is designed to produce more robust final answers by leveraging agent-specific performance signals.

Experimental results substantiate the efficacy of these innovations. On six challenging benchmarks, A-HMAD consistently outperforms both single-model baselines and the original multi-agent debate framework, achieving notable gains in accuracy and substantial reductions in factual errors ([Abstract]). Ablation studies further demonstrate the individual contributions of agent heterogeneity, additional debate rounds, and the learned consensus module ([Abstract]). Qualitative analyses reveal that specialized agents can identify and correct errors overlooked by others, supporting the hypothesis that diversity and adaptivity are critical for robust LLM-based reasoning ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

The implications of A-HMAD extend to domains where information integrity is critical. As highlighted by recent reports, the dangers of AI-generated misinformation—including in political and health contexts—underscore the necessity of architectures that can systematically reduce hallucinations and improve factual reliability ([Filtered Real-World Context]). By integrating specialized expertise, adaptive coordination, and learned consensus, A-HMAD represents a significant step toward more interpretable and pedagogically reliable AI systems ([Abstract]).

For a comprehensive technical exposition of the A-HMAD framework, its empirical evaluation, and ablation analyses, readers are encouraged to consult the full research paper ([Citation]). The paper provides detailed methodologies and results that underpin these findings and offers a foundation for further research into robust, truth-seeking AI systems.

original_title: New system efficiently explains AI judgments in realtime
abstract: Model-agnostic explanation methods are essential for interpreting machine learning models, but suffer from prohibitive computational costs that scale with the number of baselines. Existing acceleration approaches either lack a theoretical base or provide no principled guidance for baseline selection. To address this gap, we present ABSQR (Amortized Baseline Selection via Rank-Revealing QR). This framework exploits the low-rank structure of value matrices to accelerate multi-baseline attribution methods. Our approach combines deterministic baseline selection via SVD-guided QR decomposition with an amortized inference mechanism that utilizes cluster-based retrieval. We reduce computational complexity from O (m • 2d) to O (k • 2d), where k ≪ m. Experiments demonstrate that ABSQR achieves a 91.2% agreement rate with full baseline methods while providing 8.5× speedup across diverse datasets. As the first acceleration approach that preserves explanation error guarantees under computational speedup, ABSQR makes the practical deployment of interpretable AI systems feasible at scale.
introduction: The deployment of AI in sensitive domains like medical diagnostics and finance has created urgent needs for interpretable decision-making and explainable AI (XAI). One classic XAI method is feature attribution, which quantifies the contribution of each input to a prediction, enabling model debugging, fairness assessment, and regulatory compliance. In particular, model-agnostic approaches have gained prominence due to their universal applicability across different architectures, providing consistent interpretations across diverse models. Among model-agnostic attribution methods, KernelSHAP and LIME estimate importance by generating local perturbations, evaluating outputs, and performing a weighted regression on the resulting data. For tabular data, using multiple baselines can capture diverse decision boundaries and provide robust explanations, but at high computational costs. The bottleneck lies in constructing the value matrix, which requires separate model evaluations for each perturbation-baseline pair. With hundreds of baselines and thousands of feature combinations, this burden can make real-time applications infeasible. Despite extensive research into acceleration techniques, existing strategies face fundamental limitations. For instance, FastSHAP generates explanations directly from data using learned models but lacks theoretical grounding, while random sampling approaches increase variance and cause inconsistent explanations. Moreover, none of the methods provide principled guidance for baseline selection. To address these challenges, we propose Amortized Baseline Selection via Rank-Revealing QR (ABSQR), a framework that accelerates multi-baseline attribution methods by exploiting the low-rank structure of the value matrix. ABSQR operates in two stages: (1) framing baseline selection as a Column Subset Selection Problem (CSSP) solved via SVD-guided Rank-Revealing QR decomposition, and (2) introducing amortized inference through precomputed coefficients for test-time retrieval via cluster-based matching. Experiments demonstrate 91.2% fidelity with an 8.5× speedup across datasets. The contributions of this work are: (1) a general acceleration framework for multi-baseline attribution methods in XAI deployment; (2) a theoretical, deterministic baseline selection method establishing the first principled acceleration setting; (3) an amortized inference strategy that bypasses test-time model evaluation; and (4) empirical validation showing 91.2% fidelity with an 8.5× speedup across datasets.

Headline:  
ABSQR Framework Advances Scalable, Principled Explainability for Model-Agnostic AI Attribution

News Article:  
The imperative for explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) in high-stakes domains such as healthcare and finance is well established, with trust in AI systems hinging on transparent, interpretable decision-making processes [Filtered Real-World Context: 1, 2, 3]. In healthcare, for example, the ability to understand and audit AI-driven predictions is directly linked to patient safety, regulatory compliance, and the broader acceptance of automated systems [Filtered Real-World Context: 2, 3]. However, the computational burden of state-of-the-art model-agnostic explanation methods has posed a significant barrier to their practical deployment at scale.

Addressing this challenge, the ABSQR (Amortized Baseline Selection via Rank-Revealing QR) framework introduces a theoretically grounded, computationally efficient approach to accelerating multi-baseline attribution methods [Abstract, Introduction]. ABSQR achieves an 8.5× speedup while maintaining a 91.2% agreement rate with full baseline methods, as demonstrated across diverse datasets [Abstract, Introduction]. The framework is the first to provide explanation error guarantees under computational acceleration, establishing a new standard for scalable, reliable XAI in real-world applications [Abstract].

At the core of the ABSQR framework is the recognition that model-agnostic attribution methods—such as KernelSHAP and LIME—require extensive model evaluations across numerous baselines and feature perturbations to generate robust explanations [Introduction]. This process, particularly for tabular data, results in the construction of a value matrix whose size and computational demands scale prohibitively with the number of baselines and feature combinations [Introduction]. Existing acceleration strategies, including FastSHAP and random sampling, either lack theoretical justification or introduce variance and inconsistency, and none provide principled guidance for baseline selection [Introduction].

ABSQR addresses these limitations through a two-stage process. First, it frames baseline selection as a Column Subset Selection Problem (CSSP), which is solved using a deterministic, SVD-guided Rank-Revealing QR decomposition [Abstract, Introduction]. This approach exploits the low-rank structure of the value matrix, enabling the selection of a representative subset of baselines that preserves the explanatory power of the full set [Abstract, Introduction]. By reducing the number of required baselines from m to k (where k ≪ m), ABSQR lowers the computational complexity from O(m • 2^d) to O(k • 2^d) [Abstract].

The second stage introduces an amortized inference mechanism. Here, ABSQR precomputes attribution coefficients and leverages cluster-based retrieval at test time, bypassing the need for repeated model evaluations [Abstract, Introduction]. This amortized strategy further accelerates inference, making real-time, large-scale deployment of model-agnostic XAI methods feasible [Abstract, Introduction].

Empirical results validate the efficacy of ABSQR, with experiments demonstrating a 91.2% fidelity—measured as agreement with full baseline methods—while achieving an 8.5× speedup across multiple datasets [Abstract, Introduction]. These results are significant in the context of sensitive domains, where explanation fidelity and computational efficiency are both non-negotiable requirements [Filtered Real-World Context: 1, 2, 3].

The ABSQR framework delivers several key contributions: (1) a general acceleration framework for multi-baseline attribution methods in XAI deployment; (2) a deterministic, theoretically justified baseline selection method, establishing the first principled acceleration setting for these methods; (3) an amortized inference strategy that eliminates the need for test-time model evaluation; and (4) empirical validation of high-fidelity, accelerated explanations across diverse datasets [Abstract, Introduction].

As the demand for trustworthy, interpretable AI continues to intensify in domains where decision transparency is paramount, the ABSQR framework represents a substantive advance in the practical deployment of model-agnostic XAI. Researchers and practitioners seeking to understand the technical underpinnings and empirical performance of ABSQR are encouraged to consult the full research paper, which provides comprehensive details on the methodology, theoretical guarantees, and experimental validation [Citation].

End of News Article.

original_title: Periodic table for AI methods aims to drive innovation
abstract:
Deep Variational Multivariate Information Bottleneck - A Framework for Variational Losses

Abstract Variational dimensionality reduction methods are widely used for their accuracy, generative capabil- ities, and robustness. We introduce a unifying framework that generalizes both such as traditional and state-of-the-art methods. The framework is based on an interpretation of the multivariate information bottleneck, trading off the information preserved in an encoder graph (defining what to compress) against that in a decoder graph (defining a generative model for data). Using this approach, we rederive existing methods, including the deep variational information bottleneck, variational autoencoders, and deep multiview information bottleneck. We naturally extend the deep variational CCA (DVCCA) family to beta-DVCCA and introduce a new method, the deep variational symmetric information bottleneck (DVSIB). DSIB, the deterministic limit of DVSIB, connects to modern contrastive learning approaches such as Barlow Twins, among others. We evaluate these methods on Noisy MNIST and Noisy CIFAR-100, showing that algorithms better matched to the structure of the problem like DVSIB and beta-DVCCA produce better latent spaces as measured by classification accuracy, dimensionality of the latent variables, sample efficiency, and consistently outperform other approaches under comparable conditions. Additionally, we benchmark against state-of-the-art models, achieving superior or competitive accuracy. Our results demonstrate that this framework can seamlessly incorporate diverse multi-view representation learning algorithms, providing a foundation for designing novel, problem-specific loss functions. Keywords: Generative Models, Dimensionality Reduction, Data Efficiency
introduction:
1 Introduction

Large dimensional multi-modal datasets are abundant in multimedia systems utilized for language modeling (Xu et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2018; Rohrbach et al., 2017; Sanabria et al., 2018; Goyal et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Hendrycks et al., 2020), neural control of behavior studies (Steinmetz et al., 2021; Urai et al., 2022; Krakauer et al., 2017; Pang et al., 2016), multi-omics approaches in systems biology (Clark et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2017; Svensson et al., 2018; Huntley et al., 2015; Lorenzi et al., 2018), and many other domains. Such data come with the curse of dimensionality, making it hard to learn the relevant statistical correlations from samples. The problem is made even harder by the data often containing information that is irrelevant to the specific questions one asks. To tackle these challenges, a myriad of supervised and unsupervised dimensionality reduction (DR) methods have emerged. By preserving certain aspects of the data while discarding the remainder, DR can decrease the complexity of the problem, yield clearer insights, and provide a foundation for more refined modeling approaches.

DR techniques span linear methods like Principal Component Analysis (PCA) (Hotelling, 1933), Partial Least Squares (PLS) (Wold et al., 2001), Canonical Correlations Analysis (CCA) (Hotelling, 1936), and regularized CCA (Vinod, 1976; Årup Nielsen et al., 1998), as well as nonlinear approaches, including Autoencoders (AE) (Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2006), Deep CCA (Andrew et al., 2013), Deep Canonical Correlated AE (Wang et al., 2015), Correlational Neural Networks (Chandar et al., 2016), Deep Generalized CCA (Benton et al., 2017), and Deep Tensor CCA (Wong et al., 2021). Of particular interest to us are variational methods, such as Variational Autoencoders (VAE) (Kingma and Welling, 2014), beta-VAE (Higgins et al., 2016), Joint Multimodal VAE (JMVAE) (Suzuki et al., 2016), Deep Variational CCA (DVCCA) (Wang et al., 2016), Deep Variational Information Bottleneck (DVIB) (Alemi et al., 2017), Variational Mixture-of-experts AE (Shi et al., 2019), and Multiview Information Bottleneck (Federici et al., 2020b). These DR methods use deep neural networks and variational approximations to learn robust and accurate representations of the data, while, at the same time, often serving as generative models for creating samples from the learned distributions.

There are many theoretical derivations and justifications for variational DR methods (Kingma and Welling, 2014; Higgins et al., 2016; Suzuki et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Karami and Schuurmans, 2021; Qiu et al., 2022; Alemi et al., 2017; Bao, 2021; Lee and Van der Schaar, 2021; Wang et al., 2019; Wan et al., 2021; Federici et al., 2020a; Huang et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2020). This diversity of derivations, while enabling adaptability, often leaves researchers with no principled ways for choosing a method for a particular application, for designing new methods with distinct assumptions, or for comparing methods to each other.

Here, we introduce the Deep Variational Multivariate Information Bottleneck (DVMIB) framework, offering a unified mathematical foundation for many variational—and deterministic—DR methods. Our framework is grounded in the multivariate information bottleneck loss function (Tishby et al., 2000; Friedman et al., 2013). This loss, amenable to approximation through upper and lower variational bounds, provides a system for implementing diverse DR variants using deep neural networks. We demonstrate the framework’s efficacy by deriving the loss functions of many existing DR methods starting from the same principles. These include well-known methods, such as AEs (Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2006), VAEs (Kingma and Welling, 2014), and state-of-the-art methods such as Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) (Radford et al., 2021) and Barlow Twins (Zbontar et al., 2021). Furthermore, our framework naturally allows the adjustment of trade-off parameters, leading to generalizations of these existing methods. For instance, we generalize DVCCA (Wang et al., 2016) to β-DVCCA. The framework further allows us to introduce and implement in software novel DR methods. We view the DVMIB framework, with its uniform information bottleneck language, conceptual clarity of translating statistical dependencies in data via graphical models of encoder and decoder structures into variational losses, the ability to unify existing approaches, and easy adaptability to new scenarios as one of the main contributions of our work.

Beyond its unifying role, our framework offers a principled approach for deriving problem-specific loss functions using domain-specific knowledge. Thus, we anticipate its application for multi-view representation learning across diverse fields. To illustrate this, we use the framework to derive a novel dimensionality reduction method, the Deep Variational Symmetric Information Bottleneck (DVSIB), which compresses two random variables into two distinct latent variables that are maximally informative about one another. This new method produces better representations of classic datasets than previous approaches. The introduction of DVSIB is another major contribution of our paper.

In summary, our paper makes the following contributions to the field:

1. Introduction of the Variational Multivariate Information Bottleneck Framework: We provide both intuitive and mathematical insights into this framework, establishing a robust foundation for further exploration.

2. Rederivation and Generalization of Existing Methods within a Common Framework: We demonstrate the versatility of our framework by systematically rederiving and generalizing various existing methods from the literature, showcasing the framework’s ability to unify diverse approaches.

3. Design of a Novel Method — Deep Variational Symmetric Information Bottleneck (DVSIB): Employing our framework, we introduce DVSIB as a new method, contributing to the growing repertoire of techniques in variational dimensionality reduction. The method constructs high-accuracy latent spaces from substantially fewer samples than comparable approaches. Additionally, its deterministic version, the Deterministic Symmetric Information Bottleneck (DSIB), can be mapped to a plethora of state-of-the-art methods including CLIP and Barlow Twins.

The paper is structured as follows: First, in Sec. 2, we introduce the underlying mathematics and an implementation of the DVMIB framework. Then, in Sec. 3, we explain how to use the framework to generate new DR methods. In this section, in Tbl. 1, we present several known and newly variational DR methods, illustrating how easily they can be derived within the framework. In the Results (Sec. 4), as a proof of concept, we first benchmark simple computational implementations of the methods in Tbl. 1 against the Noisy MNIST dataset (Sec. 4.1). We then examine whether the trends observed in Noisy MNIST hold for more complex datasets, i.e., Noisy CIFAR-100, and more complex neural network architectures, namely CNNs (Sec. 4.2). Next, we demonstrate how the framework extends to more advanced, state-of-the-art methods (Sec. 4.3). The Appendices provide a detailed treatment of all terms in the different loss functions introduced (Appx. A). Appendix B.2 discusses auxiliary private variable models. Additional details, including visualizations, and a comprehensive performance analysis of many methods on Noisy MNIST are available in Appx. C. Further results for Noisy CIFAR-100 are provided in Appx. D.

original_title: New method improves the reliability of statistical estimations
abstract: Estimating associations between spatial covariates and responses — rather than merely predicting responses — is central to environmental science, epidemiology, and economics. For instance, public health officials might be interested in whether air pollution has a strictly positive association with a health outcome, and the mag- nitude of any effect. Standard machine learning methods often provide accurate predictions but offer limited insight into covariate-response relationships. And we show that existing methods for constructing confidence (or credible) intervals for associations can fail to provide nominal coverage in the face of model misspecifi- cation and nonrandom locations — despite both being essentially always present in spatial problems. We introduce a method that constructs valid frequentist confi- dence intervals for associations in spatial settings. Our method requires minimal assumptions beyond a form of spatial smoothness and a homoskedastic Gaussian error assumption. In particular, we do not require model correctness or covariate overlap between training and target locations. Our approach is the first to guarantee nominal coverage in this setting and outperforms existing techniques in both real and simulated experiments. Our confidence intervals are valid in finite samples when the noise of the Gaussian error is known, and we provide an asymptotically consistent estimation procedure for this noise variance when it is unknown.
introduction:
Introduction

Scientists and social scientists often seek to understand the direction and magnitude of associations in settings where variables vary spatially. And since these associations are often used to inform policy, communicating uncertainties is crucial. Example associations of interest include the relationship between aerosol concentrations and regional precipitation changes [76], the link between proximity to major highways and the prevalence of dementia [35], and the link between air pollution exposure and birth weight [34]. In each case, the covariates (e.g., aerosol concentrations, proximity to highways, and air pollution) may be viewed as functions of spatial location. Moreover, scientists often have data at some spatial locations but want to infer associations at others. For instance, a country might measure birth weight and air pollution at the municipal level for some municipalities and wish to understand their relationship in municipalities without data.

Our goal in the present work is to provide valid and useful confidence intervals for an estimator of these associations when (i) the underlying model may be misspecified and (ii) inference is needed at spatial locations that may differ from those in the observed data. First we argue that existing methods do not already solve this problem. In particular, we make this argument in turn for modern flexible machine learning methods, linear methods, spatial regression methods, and debiasing approaches.

∗Equal Contribution

Flexible Machine Learning Methods. A priori, we might expect a nonlinear relationship between air pollution (as a covariate) and birth weight (as the response). A natural idea is to fit a flexible model — such as a (deep) Gaussian process [52, 15], transformer [70, 33], or XGBoost [10]. These methods can achieve high predictive accuracy, but the methods on their own often lack interpretability and do not immediately yield conclusions about covariate-response associations [57, 17]. Researchers have proposed a number of post hoc interpretability methods, such as Shapley values [61, 38], LIME [53], partial dependence plots [20] and Accumulated Local Effects plots [3]. These methods work directly with the fitted model and can be used to describe associations between individual covariates and model predictions. And because they are interpreting the fitted model (rather than the response itself), there is no need (or mechanism) to quantify uncertainties due to insufficient data. When the fitted model closely approximates the true response, one can view the output of these interpretability methods as describing the relationship between the covariates and response.

In many applications (e.g., in engineering, advertising, and marketing), the available data often contains enough information to be confident that the highly flexible machine learning model ap- proximates the response everywhere of interest. Conversely, in many applications in the sciences and social sciences (such as those cited above), there is often insufficient data to confidently fit a nonparametric or high-dimensional model closely to the true (latent) response everywhere relevant. In particular, in many spatial problems, there is often not enough information for a flexible method to reconstruct the response well in many spatial locations of interest. Nonetheless, we might think there could still be sufficient data to capture associations — and, by quantifying uncertainty, we can check our confidence in any conclusions. The discrepancy between applications that are data-rich and data-poor in this sense can help explain the phenomenon observed by Rudin [57]: that post hoc approximations of black-box models may be worse than fitting interpretable models directly. For example, as illustrated in a real-life recidivism case, a linear model used to approximate a black-box model can seriously misrepresent key relationships — including that between race and recidivism.

Ordinary Least Squares. So it seems natural to choose an appropriate interpretable model (rather than post hoc method) and quantify its uncertainty. Buja et al. [7] demonstrated that (directly-fit, not post hoc) linear models can be used to interpretably summarize associations even in the face of potentially nonlinear relationships (i.e., under misspecification). This observation helps explain why all of the applied studies cited above use linear regression.2 Moreover, ordinary least squares (OLS) comes equipped with classical confidence intervals. However, when the linear model is misspecified, Buja et al. [7] notes that the OLS estimator depends on the covariate values, and it follows that classical confidence intervals are valid only at the observed (training) values. Since we’re interested in valid intervals at other locations, which will have different covariate values, we need another approach. The sandwich estimator [30, 77, 78, 7] offers valid intervals under misspecification, but only if all covariates are drawn from a single distribution, which isn’t the case in our setting where we want to draw inferences at different locations from where we observe data. Thus, even though OLS provides interpretable estimates, it does not solve our key challenge: constructing valid confidence intervals in the common scenario where both (i) the linear model is misspecified and (ii) inference is needed at spatial locations that may differ from those in the observed data.

Spatial Regression Methods. One might hope that a method specifically tailored for spatial regression could address these issues. Generalized least squares (GLS) regression [2] is designed to handle spatial correlation in residuals [13, pp. 22–24], but it does not address the bias introduced by misspecification and nonrandom locations. Bayesian spatial models, such as those based on Gaussian processes, are also common, but their credible intervals tend to underestimate uncertainty in the presence of both misspecification and nonrandom locations [74, 42].

Debiasing Approaches. Another natural idea is to construct a debiased estimator of the association, and then account for the variance of this debiasing procedure. Importance weighting methods from the covariate shift literature [62] pursue this goal by reweighting each observation according to an estimated density ratio between target and source covariate distributions, aiming to remove bias from distribution shift. Semiparametric inference in partially linear models (e.g., [55, 54, 11]) takes a related approach: it first fits a flexible model to capture variation explained by a “nuisance” parameter (in our case, the spatial location) and then constructs a debiased estimator of the target association by regressing out the variation explained by this nuisance, so that the remaining signal reflects the

2In fact, Castro Torres and Akbaritabar [9, Figure 1] surveyed quantitative methods in papers up to 2022 and found that over half of those in fields such as agricultural science, social sciences, and health sciences reported results from a linear model.

direct relationship between the covariate and the response. However, these methods face two key limitations. First, they assume that the debiasing step can perfectly remove bias, which need not be true (especially in finite samples). Second, their validity relies on the assumption that observations are drawn independently and identically. And this assumption rarely holds in spatial applications, where locations are fixed and often spread unevenly due to physical, logistical, or policy constraints. We discuss these and related approaches in more detail in Appendix A.

Our Contribution. In what follows, we show through real and simulated experiments (Section 4) that existing approaches can yield confidence intervals with coverage far below the nominal level. Our principal contribution is to introduce the first method for constructing confidence intervals in spatial associations that guarantees frequentist coverage at the nominal level even when the underlying model is misspecified and inference is required at fixed, nonrandom locations that differ from those observed. We are able to account for misspecification and nonrandom locations simultaneously by making more spatially appropriate assumptions than prior work. In particular, prior work relies on a (spatially inappropriate) assumption of independent and identically distribution data; we instead assume the response is a smooth function of space observed with homoskedastic Gaussian noise. When the variance of this noise is known, our confidence intervals are valid in finite samples. To address the common case where the variance is unknown, we provide an asymptotically consistent estimator for it. In our experiments, our method is the only one that consistently attains nominal coverage (or even comes close).

previous_generated_article:
Headline:  
New Method Guarantees Valid Confidence Intervals for Spatial Associations in Environmental and Health Research

News Article:  
Ambient air pollution is a well-established driver of adverse health outcomes, including increased rates of respiratory disease, cardiovascular events, and cancer, as documented by the World Health Organization and recent epidemiological studies [Filtered Real-World Context]. Quantifying the association between spatially varying covariates—such as air pollution levels—and health responses is central to public health, environmental science, and economics. Policymakers and scientists require not only accurate predictions but also robust estimates of the direction and magnitude of these associations, along with reliable measures of uncertainty, to inform interventions and regulatory decisions [Abstract], [Introduction].

Despite the proliferation of flexible machine learning models and spatial regression techniques, existing methods for constructing confidence or credible intervals for spatial associations often fail to provide nominal coverage in the presence of model misspecification and nonrandom spatial sampling—conditions that are nearly ubiquitous in real-world spatial data [Abstract], [Introduction]. The newly introduced method, detailed in the referenced research paper [Citation], addresses this persistent gap by constructing valid frequentist confidence intervals for associations in spatial settings, requiring only spatial smoothness and a homoskedastic Gaussian error assumption.

The technical challenge arises from the fact that standard machine learning approaches, including deep Gaussian processes, transformers, and XGBoost, are optimized for predictive accuracy but do not directly yield interpretable or uncertainty-quantified estimates of covariate-response associations [Introduction]. Post hoc interpretability tools such as Shapley values, LIME, and partial dependence plots operate on the fitted model rather than the underlying response, and thus do not account for uncertainty due to limited or non-overlapping data. This limitation is particularly acute in scientific and social science applications, where data sparsity and spatial heterogeneity preclude the confident application of highly flexible models across all locations of interest [Introduction].

Linear models, particularly ordinary least squares (OLS), are widely used for their interpretability and classical confidence intervals. However, as Buja et al. [7] and the present work note, OLS intervals are only valid at observed covariate values and fail to provide valid inference at unobserved spatial locations, especially under model misspecification. The sandwich estimator extends validity under misspecification but assumes covariates are drawn from a single distribution, which is not the case when inference is required at new spatial locations [Introduction].

Spatial regression methods, such as generalized least squares (GLS) and Bayesian spatial models based on Gaussian processes, are designed to address spatial correlation but do not resolve the bias introduced by model misspecification and nonrandom sampling. Bayesian credible intervals, in particular, tend to underestimate uncertainty under these conditions [Introduction]. Debiasing approaches, including importance weighting and semiparametric inference in partially linear models, attempt to correct for distributional shifts but rely on assumptions—such as perfect debiasing and independent, identically distributed observations—that are rarely satisfied in spatial applications [Introduction].

The method introduced in the referenced paper departs from these prior approaches by making spatially appropriate assumptions: the response is modeled as a smooth function of space, observed with homoskedastic Gaussian noise. Crucially, the method does not require model correctness or covariate overlap between training and target locations. When the noise variance is known, the constructed confidence intervals are valid in finite samples; when unknown, the authors provide an asymptotically consistent estimator for the variance [Abstract], [Introduction]. Empirical results from both real and simulated experiments demonstrate that this method is the only one among those tested that consistently achieves nominal coverage, even when both model misspecification and nonrandom spatial sampling are present [Abstract].

This methodological advance has direct implications for research domains where spatial associations underpin policy and health interventions. For example, studies linking air pollution exposure to birth weight or proximity to highways to dementia prevalence require inference at spatial locations where data may be sparse or absent [Introduction]. The ability to construct valid confidence intervals under minimal and spatially realistic assumptions enhances the reliability of scientific conclusions and the robustness of policy recommendations.

Researchers and practitioners engaged in spatial data analysis, particularly in environmental health and epidemiology, are encouraged to consult the full research paper [Citation] for a comprehensive exposition of the method, theoretical guarantees, and empirical validation. The paper provides the technical foundation necessary to implement these advances in practice and to critically assess the limitations of existing approaches in spatial association studies.

End of News Article.

original_title: AI can pick up cultural values by mimicking how kids learn
abstract:
Constructing a universal moral code for artificial intelligence (AI) is challenging because human cultures have different values, norms, and social practices. We

therefore argue that AI systems should adapt to culture based on observation: Just

as a child raised in a particular culture learns the specific values, norms, and behav-
introduction:
1 Introduction

A formidable challenge in the field of artificial intelligence (AI) is endowing AI agents with the richness and depth of values and behaviors that characterize human cultures and their decision-making tendencies. State-of-the-art AI systems today [1–3] are based on large-language models (LLMs), which are neural networks with billions of parameters trained on data harvested from across the internet, leading

Data availability statement: Code and data for replicating the findings in this article are available at: https:// github.com/JasmineLi-805/moral-ai-irl.

to a “one-size-fits-all” (rather than pluralistic) AI system [4,5] (see also [6,7]). In this article, we propose that rather than attempting to handcraft or learn a universal code of values and behaviors for AI, AI agents should implicitly learn them by being embedded in the human culture in which they are operating [8]. We use the term “culture” here to refer to the dynamic values, norms, and behaviors shared by a social group [9], acknowledging that it is a dynamic phenomenon—a cultural cycle in which humans continuously shape and adopt values and behaviors [10,11]. The approach we propose would endow AI agents with the ability to deal with such cul- tural dynamics in a manner that is tailored to the culture or social group in which the AI agent was “raised.” This recognizes the fact that what is acceptable or norma- tive in one culture may not be acceptable in another [12], necessitating AI systems that are capable of adapting to the values and behavioral tendencies of the culture in which they have been deployed. This will be challenging for AI and some intellec- tual humility is demanded, in as much as even humans, the creators of the AI, have a hard time appreciating and accepting the values and behaviors of cultures different from their own [12,13]. Might future AI surpass humans in such flexibility?

Funding: This work was supported by the Templeton World Charity Foundation (RPNR, ANM), the National Science Foundation EFRI Grant no. 2223495 (RPNR) and Grant no. 2230466 (KR, ANM), a UW + Amazon Science Hub grant (RPNR), a grant from the Bezos Family Foundation (ANM), and the Cherng Jia & Elizabeth Yun Hwang Professorship (RPNR). https://www.templetonworldcharity.org/ https://www.nsf.gov/ https://www.sciencehub.uw.edu/ https://www.bezosfamilyfoundation.org/ The funders did not play any role in the study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.

Competing interests: The authors have declared that no competing interests exist.

1.1 Altruism as a cultural value for AI to learn

In this article, we focus on the human tendency to act in a way that benefits others, even if it comes at a cost to oneself, often labeled “altruistic behavior.” For example, a person might give precious resources (such as food, money, or time) to someone else without directly benefiting themselves. In cases of extreme altruism, these acts can be directed towards strangers even in situations when the actor has no expectancy for reciprocal action or reward. There are significant between-country differences in altruistic tendencies across industrialized nations [14], small-scale traditional cultures [15], and even in children as a function of cultural background [16], motivating the need to investigate whether an AI system could learn altruistic behaviors from observation.

Our approach draws on evidence about how neural mechanisms in the brain support normative social behavior: the same reward systems thought to be involved in reinforcement-based learning are also harnessed to learn the dominant norms and values within a society [17–19]. The resulting system governs human cultural expectations and ways of interacting with others [20,21]. To learn what the accepted behavior is in any given context, the brain must first develop expectation rules about events and learn internal “value systems,” including the benefits of certain behav- iors as well as the costs of deviating from the norm. For example, by perceiving or producing altruistic behaviors within a culture—such as giving others items of value or otherwise acting to benefit others even at a cost to oneself—a child or an ado- lescent may learn to connect, in a certain context, a “positive” value with behaving altruistically [16,22].

There has been considerable AI research on using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) and imitation learning to mimic human actions [23–29]. More recently, IRL has been used to learn human value systems in specific contexts such as route choice modeling [30], and multi-objective IRL/RL methods have been proposed for

human value alignment [31–34]. Here, we use IRL to address the question: Can an AI agent learn cultural values and behavioral tendencies for specific social scenarios by observing the behavior of humans from different cultural groups, and then generalize this learning to a new context?

1.2 Using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to train AI on human culture

IRL is a technique developed in the field of machine learning [23,26,28] to enable an AI agent to learn a new skill from human demonstrations; rather than simply mimicking the exact actions of the human, IRL attempts to learn a “reward function” from the human demonstrations. The reward function assigns positive or negative values (“rewards” or “penal- ties”) to particular features of the observed demonstration. By learning an appropriate reward function that is aligned with the presumed reward function being used by the human in the demonstrations, the AI agent is able to imitate the human behavior and generalize beyond simple mimicry of human actions.

In this article, we show how IRL can be used to train AI agents that can infer and learn the reward functions of humans belonging to a particular cultural group. The learned reward function assigns specific reward values (positive or negative) to situations in which the AI agent may find itself. By choosing actions that optimize total expected reward based on its learned reward function, the AI agent can behave in a manner that is attuned to the human culture in which it is embed- ded. A major advantage of this approach is that the agent can assign reward values to scenarios that are related to but not exactly the same as previously encountered scenarios. This allows the agent to generalize its decision-making ability, including those involving societal values and behavioral tendencies, to novel situations, emulating the first- and second- order generalization children demonstrate based on cultural learning and experience [16,35,36].

To test this approach, we collected data from human participants playing an online game involving simple forms of altruism and fairness, both of which have been regarded as important components of human morality and culture [37–42]. We recruited participants with cultural backgrounds associated with different altruistic behaviors [43], specifically, US participants who identified as White or as Latino (we use the term “Latino” to denote anyone born in or with ancestors from Latin America, regardless of gender).

Our hypothesis regarding differences in altruistic behavior is motivated by prior work showing that, within the same nation-state (here, the US), Latinos tend to be more collectivistic [44,45] and tend to have a more interdependent self- concept than Whites, who are generally characterized as being more individualistic and prone to seeing themselves as independent of others [46–48]. Whereas individualistic cultures tend to emphasize personal achievement and autonomy, collectivist cultures often prioritize group harmony and the achievements and welfare of others over personal gains. We hypothesize that, within the novel context of our online “world,” Latinos will exhibit more altruistic behavior than Whites. That is, subgroups of people within the US share cultural tendencies that they may bring to an online game, which we hypothesize can be inferred through IRL by an AI agent.

Note that rather than using data from human adults who make explicit value judgments, as in the case of the Moral Machines experiment involving the ethics of self-driving cars [49] or the trolley problem [50,51] (see also [52]), or data from descriptive judgments of scenarios presenting values and norms, as in the Delphi experiment [53] (see also [4,5]), our approach seeks to emulate the way a human child absorbs values and norms implicitly from observation and inter- actions with people in their culture [16,54]. Our approach shares similarities with previous work in reinforcement learn- ing [55,56] and brain-inspired affective learning [57] showing how an AI agent could learn ethical characteristics such as altruism, empathy, and sympathy; these approaches did not train their AI agent on human data (as in our study here) and did not focus on cultural differences. Finally, our online game scenario includes an element of fairness, in addition to altruism: the “privileged” player has to endure some costs to help the other player who has been placed in an unfair and potentially less rewarding circumstance. We hypothesize that the reward functions inferred by IRL from human data may implicitly capture this aspect of the game scenario.

To summarize, this article tests the following hypotheses:

H1: Cultural background and exposure to altruistic behavior may influence participants’ tendencies to display altruistic

behavior.

H2: An AI agent using inverse reinforcement learning can learn culturally attuned, quantifiable altruistic behaviors and

values for specific social scenarios by observing the behavior of humans from different cultural groups.

H3: An AI agent can generalize learned social values and behavior to a new context.

The remainder of the article focuses on the above hypothesis and presents our results. We first describe our experimen- tal studies with humans and present results showing that an AI agent can learn from observing the behavior of a particular cultural group (Latinos or Whites) an IRL reward function capturing the altruistic tendencies of that group. We then show how this learning can generalize to new problems involving other altruistic decision making. Taken together, these results, though obtained from a simple online game scenario, point the way towards future AI agents that could become attuned to the culture in which they are operating by continually learning and adjusting their behavior from observing and interacting with humans within that culture.

Headline:  
Inverse Reinforcement Learning Enables AI to Acquire Culturally Specific Altruistic Behaviors

News Article:  
The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into diverse societies has exposed the limitations of “one-size-fits-all” models, particularly in contexts where cultural values and moral norms diverge. Recent analyses highlight that AI adoption patterns and societal trust in AI are closely linked to the alignment of AI behavior with local moral values, with cultural insensitivity leading to diminished trust and suboptimal societal outcomes [Filtered Real-World Context: 1, 3]. This underscores the technical and ethical imperative for AI systems to adapt to the dynamic values, norms, and behaviors of the cultures in which they are deployed.

A recent study addresses this challenge by proposing that AI agents should learn cultural values and behavioral tendencies through observation, rather than relying on handcrafted or universal codes of conduct [Abstract, Introduction]. The authors argue that, analogous to the way a child internalizes the values and norms of their surrounding culture, AI systems should be embedded within the human culture they serve, learning implicitly from observed human behavior. This approach is positioned as a response to the limitations of current large-language models (LLMs), which are trained on heterogeneous internet-scale data and thus lack the capacity for cultural specificity [Introduction].

The technical core of the study is the application of inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to enable AI agents to infer and internalize the reward functions that underlie culturally specific human behaviors. IRL, a machine learning technique, allows an AI agent to learn a reward function from human demonstrations, rather than merely mimicking observed actions [Introduction, 1.2]. The learned reward function assigns positive or negative values to features of observed behavior, enabling the agent to generalize its decision-making to novel scenarios that share structural similarities with previously encountered situations [1.2]. This generalization is analogous to the first- and second-order generalization observed in human cultural learning [1.2].

The study operationalizes this framework by focusing on altruistic behavior—a core component of human morality that exhibits significant cross-cultural variation [1.1]. The experimental design involved collecting behavioral data from human participants with distinct cultural backgrounds (specifically, US participants identifying as White or Latino) as they engaged in an online game involving altruism and fairness [1.2]. Prior research cited by the authors indicates that Latinos in the US tend to exhibit more collectivistic and interdependent self-concepts, while Whites are characterized as more individualistic [1.2]. The hypothesis was that these cultural tendencies would manifest in the game, with Latinos displaying higher levels of altruistic behavior.

The AI agent, trained via IRL on the behavioral data from each cultural group, was able to infer reward functions that captured the group-specific altruistic tendencies [1.2]. Notably, the agent was able to generalize these learned values to new contexts within the game, demonstrating the capacity to adapt its behavior in a manner attuned to the cultural background of the observed group [1.2]. This approach diverges from prior work that relies on explicit value judgments or descriptive scenario-based data, instead emulating the implicit, observation-driven learning process characteristic of human cultural development [1.2].

The implications of this research are significant for the development of AI systems that are both technically robust and culturally adaptive. The findings suggest that AI agents can be endowed with the ability to learn and generalize culturally specific values and behaviors, addressing a key limitation of current LLM-based systems [Introduction]. This has direct relevance for the deployment of AI in multicultural societies, where the failure to account for local norms can erode trust and hinder adoption [Filtered Real-World Context: 1, 3]. Furthermore, the study’s methodology aligns with emerging frameworks for evaluating the ethical-moral intelligence of AI models, which emphasize the need for context-sensitive, culturally grounded evaluation metrics [Filtered Real-World Context: 2].

The study’s results, while derived from a controlled online game scenario, provide a technical foundation for future research on culturally adaptive AI. The authors acknowledge the complexity of cultural dynamics and the intellectual humility required, given that even humans struggle to fully appreciate the values of cultures different from their own [Introduction]. The prospect that AI agents might eventually surpass humans in cultural flexibility is raised as an open question for further investigation.

For researchers and practitioners seeking a detailed account of the experimental design, data, and technical implementation, the full research paper and associated code are available at https://github.com/JasmineLi-805/moral-ai-irl [Introduction]. The paper offers a comprehensive resource for those interested in the intersection of machine learning, cultural psychology, and AI ethics, and provides the empirical and methodological details necessary to replicate and extend these findings.

End of News Article.

Prior research indicates that Latinos in the US tend to exhibit more collectivistic and interdependent self-concepts, while Whites are generally characterized as more individualistic [Introduction, 1.2]. The study hypothesized that these cultural tendencies would manifest in the game, with Latinos displaying higher levels of altruistic behavior.

The AI agent, trained via IRL on the observed behaviors of each cultural group, was able to infer reward functions that captured the group-specific altruistic tendencies [Introduction, 1.2]. Notably, the agent demonstrated the capacity to generalize these learned values to novel scenarios, emulating the first- and second-order generalization observed in human cultural learning [Introduction, 1.2]. This result supports the hypothesis that AI agents can acquire culturally attuned, quantifiable altruistic behaviors and values for specific social scenarios by observing human behavior, and can extend this learning to new contexts [Introduction, 1.2].

The implications of this research are significant for the development of AI systems that are both technically robust and socially adaptive. By enabling AI agents to learn and generalize cultural values through observation, the approach addresses the limitations of universalist value alignment strategies and offers a pathway toward AI systems that are responsive to the dynamic, context-dependent nature of human morality [Abstract; Introduction]. This is particularly salient given the documented societal consequences of cultural insensitivity in AI, including reduced trust, fairness concerns, and the risk of exacerbating social inequities [Filtered Real-World Context: 1, 2, 3, 4].

The study’s methodology diverges from prior work that relies on explicit value judgments or descriptive scenario-based data, instead emulating the implicit, observational learning processes characteristic of human cultural transmission [Introduction, 1.2]. This distinction is technically significant, as it enables the AI agent to capture not only explicit norms but also the nuanced, context-dependent behaviors that define cultural identity.

For researchers and practitioners seeking to advance the state of culturally adaptive AI, the full research paper provides detailed experimental protocols, data, and code for replication [Citation]. The findings offer a foundation for future work on embedding AI agents within dynamic cultural cycles, with the potential to surpass human flexibility in adapting to diverse value systems [Abstract; Introduction].

To access the complete methodology, results, and technical resources underpinning these findings, readers are encouraged to consult the original research paper and its associated code repository at https://github.com/JasmineLi-805/moral-ai-irl.

End of News Article.
